5.7.a.info:
DHCP snooping is a Layer 2 security feature on Cisco switches that acts as a mini-firewall for DHCP messages. It categorizes each switch port as trusted or untrusted, then inspects every DHCP frame that crosses the switch and drops any message that doesn't belong on that port type.

The core rule: DHCP server messages (Offer, ACK, NAK) are only permitted from trusted ports. DHCP client messages (Discover, Request, Release, Decline) are permitted from any port. If an attacker plugs a rogue DHCP server into a user-facing (untrusted) port and that server tries to send an Offer, the switch drops the Offer before it reaches any client.

Why this matters: Without DHCP snooping, a rogue DHCP server can hand out false configurations — assigning itself as the default gateway so all victim traffic flows through the attacker (classic man-in-the-middle). The attacker sees every packet, can modify DNS responses, and can inject malicious redirects. DHCP snooping kills this attack at the switch port.

Secondary benefit — the binding table: As legitimate DHCP transactions complete, the switch records each (MAC, IP, VLAN, port, lease) tuple in the DHCP snooping binding table. This table is the foundation that DAI and IP Source Guard build on — both features read the binding table to validate traffic.

Verification: show ip dhcp snooping displays the global and per-VLAN state. show ip dhcp snooping binding shows learned client bindings.

hack.memory: DHCP snooping = a bouncer at every switch port checking DHCP IDs. USERS can ASK for an address (Discover/Request allowed). USERS cannot HAND OUT addresses (Offer/ACK blocked). Only the real DHCP server (on a TRUSTED port) is allowed to hand out addresses. Bonus: it writes down every lease in a binding table — that table becomes the truth source for DAI and IP Source Guard.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: connect a legitimate DHCP server on Gi0/1 and a rogue DHCP server on Gi0/5. Without snooping, let a client request an address — sometimes the rogue wins the race. Now enable snooping globally and per VLAN, mark Gi0/1 trusted, leave Gi0/5 untrusted. Repeat the test — the rogue's Offer is dropped every time. Then run 'show ip dhcp snooping binding' to see the legit lease recorded.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53 walks through DHCP snooping with a rogue-server scenario. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30 covers the full DHCP attack surface. Exam framing: 'Which feature blocks rogue DHCP servers?' = DHCP snooping. 'What feature builds the binding table DAI uses?' = DHCP snooping. Always the foundation — learn it first.

5.7.a.info:
Every port on a DHCP-snooping-enabled switch is either trusted or untrusted. This binary state controls exactly which DHCP message types the switch will forward on that port.

Trusted port — faces a legitimate DHCP server or is an uplink/trunk toward a switch that faces a server. ALL DHCP message types pass: Discover, Offer, Request, ACK, NAK, Release, Decline. You manually flag these ports with ip dhcp snooping trust at interface config. Typical trusted ports: the uplink to the DHCP server's switch, trunk ports between access/distribution switches, and ports directly facing the DHCP server itself.

Untrusted port (default) — every port becomes untrusted the moment you enable snooping on its VLAN. Only client DHCP messages are allowed (Discover, Request, Release, Decline). If a server-side message (Offer, ACK, NAK) appears on an untrusted port, the switch drops the packet and usually logs the event. This is what kills rogue-DHCP-server attacks.

Gotcha: It's easy to forget to trust the uplink toward the real DHCP server — if you don't, the server's Offer gets dropped along with the rogue's. Symptom: clients can't obtain addresses at all after snooping is enabled. Fix: ip dhcp snooping trust on the uplink facing the legitimate server.

Verify port trust state: show ip dhcp snooping lists trusted and untrusted interfaces. Every port that isn't explicitly trusted is untrusted — confirm the uplink side is trusted.

hack.memory: Trust = server side. Untrusted = user side. If you forget to TRUST the uplink to the real DHCP server, you just blocked YOUR OWN DHCP — everyone's offline. Classic self-inflicted outage.
hack.practice: Draw the topology: DHCP server → SwA Gi0/1 → trunk → SwB Gi0/24 → user on SwB Gi0/5. Label which port needs 'ip dhcp snooping trust': SwA Gi0/1 (faces server), SwA trunk port, SwB trunk port. Leave SwB Gi0/5 untrusted. Walk a DHCP Discover and Offer through the topology — verify every hop's trust state allows the message.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Most common exam scenario: a snooping config that blocks legit DHCP. You're shown the running-config and asked what's wrong — answer is almost always 'uplink not trusted.'

5.7.a.info:
The DHCP snooping binding table is a list the switch builds automatically by watching successful DHCP transactions on untrusted ports. Every time a client successfully leases an address, the switch records a row.

Each binding row contains:

  • MAC address — the client's hardware address
  • IP address — what the DHCP server assigned
  • VLAN — the client's access VLAN
  • Interface — the switch port the client is on
  • Lease time — how long the binding is valid (matches the DHCP lease)
  • Binding type — dhcp-snooping (learned) or static (manual)

Why this table is the most important artifact in L2 security: DAI reads it to validate every ARP reply. IP Source Guard reads it to validate every source IP on a port. Without this table, neither DAI nor IPSG has any data and both end up dropping legitimate traffic.

Persistence: The binding table lives in RAM by default and is lost on reboot. To survive reboots, save it: ip dhcp snooping database tftp://10.0.0.10/bindings.db (or flash:bindings.db). The switch rewrites the file periodically and reloads it on boot.

Static bindings (for hosts with fixed IPs that don't DHCP): ip source binding aaaa.bbbb.cccc vlan 10 10.0.0.50 interface Gi0/5 — manually adds a row so DAI and IPSG treat the static host as legitimate.

Verify: show ip dhcp snooping binding.

hack.memory: Binding table = the switch's ledger of who-has-which-IP-on-which-port. Every DHCP lease writes one row. DAI reads it to decide 'is this ARP real?' IPSG reads it to decide 'is this source IP allowed on this port?' No table = no DAI, no IPSG. Save with 'ip dhcp snooping database' or it dies on reboot.
hack.practice: Enable snooping, let three clients DHCP. Run 'show ip dhcp snooping binding' — three rows, one per client. Now manually add a static binding for a printer with a fixed IP: 'ip source binding 1111.2222.3333 vlan 10 10.0.0.200 interface Gi0/8'. Verify the row appears. Configure 'ip dhcp snooping database flash:snoop.db' and reload — verify bindings survive.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam question set: 'What table does DAI use?' = DHCP snooping binding table. 'How do static-IP hosts get DAI/IPSG?' = static binding or ARP ACL. Expect to see 'show ip dhcp snooping binding' output on the exam.

5.7.b.info:
The global command ip dhcp snooping (entered in global config) is the master switch for the feature. Without this command, no per-VLAN or per-interface snooping configuration does anything — the feature is completely off.

What it does by itself: activates the snooping subsystem and prepares the switch to begin building the binding table and enforcing trust rules once you also turn on snooping per VLAN. On its own, this single command changes nothing about traffic forwarding — you still need step 2 (per-VLAN) for enforcement to actually happen.

Why the two-step design: Cisco split enable into global + per-VLAN so you can deploy snooping incrementally. You turn it on globally once, then roll it out VLAN by VLAN to catch problems on a small set of users before breaking DHCP network-wide.

Common mistake: typing only ip dhcp snooping and expecting protection. With no VLAN enabled, snooping is inert — the feature is loaded but not enforcing anything. Always pair with ip dhcp snooping vlan [list].

Verification: show ip dhcp snooping — the first line states whether snooping is globally enabled (Switch DHCP snooping is enabled) and lists the VLANs where operational snooping is active.

hack.memory: Global enable = flip the MAIN breaker. Without this command, nothing snooping-related matters — the feature is powered off. But flipping only this breaker still doesn't do anything until you also turn on at least one VLAN.
hack.practice: On a lab switch, type only 'ip dhcp snooping' and nothing else. Test with a rogue DHCP server — it still works (nothing is being filtered). Now add 'ip dhcp snooping vlan 10' and the rogue is blocked. This proves the global command alone is not protection.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Simple recall: this is step 1 of 3. The exam expects you to know all three steps (global, VLAN, trust) in order.

5.7.b.info:
ip dhcp snooping vlan [vlan-list] activates snooping for specific VLANs. This is the command that actually starts enforcement — without it, no DHCP packets are inspected even if the global enable is on.

Syntax:

Switch(config)# ip dhcp snooping vlan 10,20,30
Switch(config)# ip dhcp snooping vlan 100-200

You can list individual VLANs, ranges, or comma-separated combinations. Only DHCP packets riding on enabled VLANs are inspected; traffic on other VLANs is forwarded without any snooping logic applied.

Key behavior: The moment a VLAN is enabled, every port carrying that VLAN becomes untrusted by default. If you forgot to mark the uplink trusted BEFORE enabling the VLAN, DHCP for that VLAN breaks immediately — the real server's Offer gets dropped along with any rogue. Safe rollout: trust the uplinks first, then enable the VLAN.

Trunk ports: On a trunk carrying VLANs 10, 20, 30, snooping only applies to DHCP in the VLANs you've enabled. If 10 and 20 are enabled but not 30, VLAN 30's DHCP is untouched on that trunk.

Option 82 on trunks: If snooping adds Option 82 on trunk ingress and your DHCP server or relay drops Option-82-tagged packets, DHCP fails. Either disable Option 82 insertion (no ip dhcp snooping information option) or trust the port (ip dhcp snooping information option allow-untrusted on the relay).

hack.memory: Global enable = power on. VLAN enable = turn on the actual camera in a specific hallway. You can power on the system without watching any hallway (useless). Rule of rollout: TRUST THE UPLINK FIRST, then enable the VLAN — otherwise you just blocked your own DHCP server.
hack.practice: Set up a lab with two VLANs (10 and 20). Enable snooping globally. Enable only VLAN 10: 'ip dhcp snooping vlan 10'. Plug a rogue into VLAN 10 — blocked. Plug a rogue into VLAN 20 — forwarded (not enforced). Now add VLAN 20 and repeat — both blocked. This cements that the VLAN command is what arms enforcement.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam traps: (1) missing VLAN enable = feature inactive. (2) VLAN enabled but uplink untrusted = legit DHCP also blocked. (3) Option 82 breaking DHCP when server doesn't expect it.

5.7.b.info:
ip dhcp snooping trust is an interface-level command that marks a port as trusted for DHCP snooping. Trusted ports allow every DHCP message type to pass; untrusted ports (the default) drop server-side messages.

Where to apply trust:

  • Port directly facing a legitimate DHCP server
  • Uplinks (access → distribution, distribution → core) where a DHCP server lives upstream
  • Trunks between switches that carry VLAN traffic toward the DHCP server
  • Port facing a DHCP relay agent (which forwards Discovers/Requests to a remote server and returns Offers/ACKs)

Where NOT to apply trust: end-user access ports, guest ports, conference room ports, printer ports, IoT device ports — anywhere a user could plug in a rogue device.

Default: Every port is untrusted once snooping is enabled on its VLAN. You explicitly opt into trust with ip dhcp snooping trust. Unset with no ip dhcp snooping trust.

Example config:

interface GigabitEthernet0/24
 description Uplink to Distribution
 ip dhcp snooping trust

Verification: show ip dhcp snooping lists trusted interfaces in a separate section. Confirm your uplinks appear there before trusting.

hack.memory: Trust = 'this port is safe, let Offers through.' Apply to uplinks and server-facing ports. Everything else (user ports) stays untrusted. Rule of thumb: if a user could plug in a laptop there, it's UNTRUSTED.
hack.practice: On a three-switch lab (access-dist-core), identify every trust point: access→dist trunk (trusted on both ends), dist→core trunk (trusted on both ends), core→DHCP server port (trusted). User ports on access switch: untrusted. Write the exact 'ip dhcp snooping trust' commands for every interface that needs it. Deploy and verify with 'show ip dhcp snooping'.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The #1 exam scenario: 'DHCP broke after snooping was enabled — why?' Answer: uplink wasn't trusted. Always, always, always trust the server-facing path first.

5.7.b.info:
DHCP Option 82 (Relay Agent Information) is a field the switch inserts into DHCP messages when snooping is enabled. It identifies which switch and which port the DHCP request came from, letting a centralized DHCP server apply per-port policies. Cisco switches insert Option 82 by default when snooping runs.

The problem: Some DHCP servers (older Microsoft DHCP, third-party appliances) receive a DHCP Discover with Option 82 and silently drop it — they don't recognize the option and consider the packet malformed. Symptom: clients suddenly can't get addresses after you enable snooping, but the binding table is empty and no errors appear on the switch.

Three fixes:

  • no ip dhcp snooping information option — disables Option 82 insertion entirely. Simplest fix. The downside is you lose the ability to do per-port policy on the DHCP server.
  • Tell the relay agent to accept Option 82 from untrusted sources: ip dhcp relay information trust-all (on the relay router).
  • Update the DHCP server to understand Option 82 (preferred if the server supports it).

On modern Cisco DHCP and Windows Server 2016+: Option 82 is generally handled fine. But in labs with older servers or Packet Tracer DHCP, you usually need to disable Option 82.

Verify: show ip dhcp snooping shows whether Option 82 insertion is enabled.

hack.memory: Snooping sticks Option 82 on every DHCP packet. Some servers hate it and silently drop — clients get no IP and the binding table stays empty. Fix: 'no ip dhcp snooping information option'. First thing to try in labs when DHCP dies after enabling snooping.
hack.practice: Enable snooping in Packet Tracer against a generic DHCP server. Watch clients fail to get IPs even though everything looks right. Run 'show ip dhcp snooping' and see Option 82 is enabled. Add 'no ip dhcp snooping information option'. Clients now get IPs. This one test cements the gotcha permanently.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53 mentions this explicitly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Classic lab-troubleshooting scenario. Memorize the command: 'no ip dhcp snooping information option'.

5.7.c.info:
The DHCP snooping binding table is the single source of truth that Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) uses to decide whether an ARP packet is legitimate or malicious. Every ARP reply that arrives on an untrusted port is checked against this table.

The validation check: When an ARP Reply enters an untrusted port, DAI extracts the sender MAC and sender IP from the ARP payload. It then looks in the binding table for a matching row. If the (MAC, IP, VLAN, port) tuple matches an existing binding, the ARP is forwarded. If any field doesn't match, the ARP is dropped and a violation is logged.

Why this stops ARP spoofing: An attacker sending gratuitous ARPs that claim 'I am 10.0.0.1' (the gateway) with their own MAC will not match any binding in the table — because the real gateway's IP-to-MAC mapping was never learned via DHCP (gateways usually have static IPs, and even if dynamic, the mapping belongs to a different port/MAC). DAI drops the forged ARP and the victim's ARP cache stays clean.

Hard dependency: No DHCP snooping → no binding table → DAI has nothing to compare against. If DAI is enabled without snooping, DAI will drop every ARP on untrusted ports because no bindings exist. This is why snooping must be turned on and given time to populate the table BEFORE enabling DAI.

Static exceptions: Hosts with static IPs (printers, servers) never DHCP, so they have no binding. Use ip source binding statements or arp access-list + ip arp inspection filter to whitelist them.

hack.memory: DAI = 'show me your lease papers.' Every ARP reply on an untrusted port has to match a row in the DHCP snooping binding table. No matching row = dropped. That's how DAI stops ARP poisoning: the attacker's forged MAC-IP pair was never in a real DHCP lease.
hack.practice: Lab: enable snooping, let clients DHCP. View the binding table. Now from an untrusted PC, send a gratuitous ARP claiming to be the gateway (use Scapy: 'sendp(Ether()/ARP(op=2, psrc="10.0.0.1", hwsrc=attacker_mac))'). Before DAI: victims' ARP caches poison. Enable DAI on the VLAN. Send the same ARP — it's dropped. Check 'show ip arp inspection statistics' — the drop counter incremented.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The exam fingerprint question: 'What must be enabled before DAI can work?' = DHCP snooping. 'What does DAI use to validate ARPs?' = the DHCP snooping binding table. Always these two answers together.

5.7.c.info:
show ip dhcp snooping binding prints the current contents of the binding table — every DHCP-learned client currently holding a valid lease.

Example output:

MacAddress          IpAddress       Lease(sec)  Type           VLAN  Interface
------------------  --------------  ----------  -------------  ----  -----------
aa:bb:cc:dd:ee:ff   10.0.0.50       86298       dhcp-snooping  10    Gi0/5
11:22:33:44:55:66   10.0.0.51       86312       dhcp-snooping  10    Gi0/6
Total number of bindings: 2

Reading the columns:

  • MacAddress — client's L2 hardware address
  • IpAddress — lease assigned by DHCP server
  • Lease(sec) — seconds remaining on the lease (counts down)
  • Type — dhcp-snooping (learned via DHCP) or static (manually added with ip source binding)
  • VLAN — client's access VLAN
  • Interface — switch port the client is on

Troubleshooting checklist when the table is empty: (1) Is snooping globally enabled? (ip dhcp snooping) (2) Is the client's VLAN enabled for snooping? (ip dhcp snooping vlan 10) (3) Is the uplink to the DHCP server trusted? (ip dhcp snooping trust) (4) Is Option 82 breaking DHCP? (no ip dhcp snooping information option) (5) Did the client actually get an IP? (check DHCP server or 'ipconfig /all' on client).

Related commands: show ip dhcp snooping (global/VLAN state), show ip dhcp snooping statistics (counters for forwarded/dropped packets), clear ip dhcp snooping binding (wipe the table, usually only for debug).

layers: show ip arp inspection (DAI uses binding)

hack.memory: 'show ip dhcp snooping binding' = the client roster. Empty table = snooping isn't catching leases (wrong VLAN, untrusted uplink, Option 82). Full table = snooping working and DAI/IPSG will have data to use.
hack.practice: Lab: enable snooping, let 3 clients DHCP, run the command — 3 rows. Now shut down the trusted uplink so the next client can't reach the DHCP server — client fails to get an IP, table stays at 3. This shows the table only grows when snooping observes a successful DHCP Ack.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam expects you to read this output and identify clients, VLANs, and ports. If they show you the table in a sim, you need to recognize what each column means.

5.7.d.info:
A DHCP starvation attack floods the DHCP server with thousands of DHCP Discover messages, each using a different spoofed source MAC address. The DHCP server treats each spoofed MAC as a new client and assigns it an IP from its pool. Within minutes, the entire DHCP pool is exhausted — and legitimate clients can no longer get addresses.

Mechanics: The attacker's tool (Yersinia, Gobbler, dhcpstarv) generates packets like:

DHCP Discover  src-MAC: 00:01:02:03:04:05  xid: 1001
DHCP Discover  src-MAC: 00:01:02:03:04:06  xid: 1002
DHCP Discover  src-MAC: 00:01:02:03:04:07  xid: 1003
... (thousands per second)

The DHCP server responds to each with an Offer and then an ACK, consuming one IP per forged MAC. A /24 pool (254 addresses) is exhausted in seconds on modern hardware.

Why it's dangerous: Denial of service is just the obvious effect. The attack often pairs with a rogue DHCP server — once the real server's pool is exhausted, the attacker's rogue server is the only one answering Discovers. New clients get the attacker as their default gateway, enabling full MITM.

Defense stack: (1) DHCP snooping rate limiting (ip dhcp snooping limit rate) caps how many DHCP packets per second a port can send — the flood triggers err-disable before the pool drains. (2) Port security limits the number of source MACs allowed on the port, preventing the MAC-spoofing loop altogether. (3) DHCP snooping validates the chaddr field matches the Ethernet source MAC (ip dhcp snooping verify mac-address).

hack.memory: DHCP starvation = 1000 fake clients, 1000 spoofed MACs, 1000 Discovers per second. Pool exhausted in seconds. Real clients starve. Often paired with a rogue server that takes over once the real one runs dry.
hack.practice: In a lab (isolated!), use Yersinia to simulate starvation against a DHCP server. Watch the address pool drain. Then enable port security with max 2 MACs and rate limiting at 10 pps on the attacker's port. Re-run the attack — port goes err-disabled immediately, pool stays intact. Understand the attack before deploying the defenses.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The exam wants you to identify the attack by its signature (many Discovers, spoofed MACs, pool exhausted) and know the defenses (rate limiting, port security, MAC verification).

5.7.d.info:
Defending against DHCP starvation is a layered problem — no single command is enough, because the attack operates on multiple vectors (flood rate, MAC spoofing, pool exhaustion). A proper defense combines three features.

Layer 1 — DHCP snooping rate limiting: Cap DHCP packets per second on untrusted ports. Normal clients send 1–4 DHCP packets during a lease lifecycle. Setting the limit to 10–20 pps catches floods without affecting normal traffic.

interface Gi0/5
 ip dhcp snooping limit rate 15

When the rate is exceeded, the port transitions to err-disabled state, halting the flood.

Layer 2 — Port security MAC limit: Port security caps the total number of MAC addresses on the port (default 1). Starvation relies on each spoofed packet using a new source MAC — but port security sees each new MAC as a violation after the max is hit.

interface Gi0/5
 switchport mode access
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security violation shutdown

Layer 3 — DHCP chaddr MAC verification: The attacker may spoof the Ethernet source MAC but reuse a single client-hardware-address (chaddr) in the DHCP payload. Or vice versa. Snooping can cross-check them:

ip dhcp snooping verify mac-address

Any packet where Ethernet source MAC ≠ chaddr is dropped.

Combined effect: Each spoofed Discover is blocked by at least one of: rate limit (flood), port security (MAC count), or MAC verification (MAC mismatch). Even if the attacker bypasses one, the others still catch it.

items: Rate limit: 10-15 pps on untrusted ports
verify mac-address: chaddr vs Ethernet src

hack.memory: Three walls: (1) rate limit — only a few DHCPs per second. (2) port security — only a few MACs per port. (3) verify mac-address — chaddr must equal Ethernet source. An attacker must defeat ALL THREE to starve the pool. Usually impossible.
hack.practice: Write all three commands from memory for a single interface. Apply them to a lab port. Run Yersinia against it — port goes err-disabled almost instantly. Now try to tune the attack (lower rate, reuse MACs) and watch how each defense catches a different attack style. This is the clearest way to see defense-in-depth.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The exam asks for the combination: 'Which features together stop starvation?' — rate limit + port security + MAC verify. Know all three.

5.7.d.info:
ip dhcp snooping limit rate [packets-per-second] is an interface-level command that caps how many DHCP packets per second a port is allowed to send. Exceed the rate, and the port goes err-disabled.

Syntax:

interface GigabitEthernet0/5
 ip dhcp snooping limit rate 15

Typical values:

  • Access ports (untrusted): 10–20 pps — a normal client sends maybe 4 DHCP packets during a lease cycle
  • Trunk ports (trusted): do NOT rate limit, or use a much higher value (100+) — legitimate trunks carry DHCP for many clients
  • Server ports: do not rate limit — the DHCP server itself sends high volumes of Offer/ACK packets

What happens at the limit: The port transitions to err-disabled, syslog fires, and all traffic on the port stops. Recovery is manual (shutdown / no shutdown) or automatic via errdisable recovery cause dhcp-rate-limit + errdisable recovery interval 300.

Implementation detail: the rate limit uses a token bucket — short bursts above the limit are tolerated briefly. But sustained traffic above the rate triggers err-disable.

Verify: show ip dhcp snooping shows rate limits per interface. show interfaces status err-disabled confirms which ports tripped.

hack.memory: Rate limit = speed bump. Normal client: 4 packets in a full lease lifecycle. Set 15 pps on access ports. Never rate limit a trunk or server port — you'll err-disable legitimate paths. Recovery: 'shut / no shut' or auto via 'errdisable recovery cause dhcp-rate-limit'.
hack.practice: Apply 'ip dhcp snooping limit rate 10' on a lab access port. Simulate a flood (Yersinia or a script that sends 50 DHCPs/sec). Port immediately goes err-disabled. Recover manually. Then enable 'errdisable recovery cause dhcp-rate-limit' with interval 30 — flood again, watch it recover after 30s. Know both recovery paths.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam fact: rate limit applies to untrusted ports. Recovery can be manual or automated via errdisable recovery.

5.7.e.info:
Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) is a Layer 2 security feature that validates ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table and drops any ARP whose claimed MAC-IP pair doesn't match a known binding. It is the primary defense against ARP spoofing / ARP poisoning, the most common LAN man-in-the-middle attack.

The attack it stops: An attacker on the same VLAN as the gateway sends gratuitous ARP replies claiming 'I am 10.0.0.1 (the gateway)' — but with the attacker's own MAC. Every host on the VLAN accepts the unsolicited reply and updates its ARP cache. Now all traffic destined for the gateway flows to the attacker, who silently relays it and reads/modifies everything.

How DAI blocks it: When an ARP packet arrives on an untrusted port, DAI extracts the sender MAC and sender IP from the ARP payload and looks them up in the DHCP snooping binding table. If the pair doesn't match any binding (MAC, IP, VLAN, interface), the ARP is dropped and a violation is logged.

Additional validation (optional):

  • ip arp inspection validate src-mac — verify Ethernet source MAC equals ARP sender MAC
  • ip arp inspection validate dst-mac — verify Ethernet destination MAC equals ARP target MAC (for replies)
  • ip arp inspection validate ip — sanity-check IPs (no 0.0.0.0, no multicast, no broadcast in ARP sender/target)

Per-VLAN activation: ip arp inspection vlan 10,20. Trust uplinks with ip arp inspection trust. Verify: show ip arp inspection, show ip arp inspection statistics.

items: Validates every ARP against binding table

hack.memory: DAI = 'prove your ARP.' Every ARP on an untrusted port is checked against the DHCP snooping binding table. Forged ARPs (attacker claiming to be the gateway) don't match any real lease → dropped. Kills ARP poisoning at the switch port.
hack.practice: Attack lab: two PCs + gateway on same VLAN. From attacker, run arpspoof or ettercap: 'arpspoof -i eth0 -t victim gateway'. Watch victim's ARP cache update with attacker's MAC for the gateway IP. Now enable DAI on the VLAN (after snooping). Repeat the attack — 'show ip arp inspection statistics' shows drops, victim's ARP cache stays clean.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam flow: DHCP snooping → binding table → DAI. Know the validation checks (src-mac, dst-mac, ip) and the per-VLAN enable command.

5.7.e.info:
DAI has no data source of its own. It depends entirely on the DHCP snooping binding table to decide whether an ARP packet is legitimate. If snooping isn't running or hasn't built any bindings yet, DAI has nothing to validate against — and its default behavior is to drop.

The operational order is mandatory:

  1. Enable DHCP snooping globally: ip dhcp snooping
  2. Enable DHCP snooping per VLAN: ip dhcp snooping vlan 10
  3. Trust the uplinks: ip dhcp snooping trust
  4. Wait for clients to DHCP (binding table populates)
  5. Verify bindings exist: show ip dhcp snooping binding
  6. Enable DAI per VLAN: ip arp inspection vlan 10
  7. Trust DAI uplinks: ip arp inspection trust

What happens if you skip snooping: Every ARP from every client on an untrusted port is dropped (no matching binding). Users lose all connectivity — they can't resolve the gateway's MAC, so they can't send any frames off-subnet.

Static-IP hosts (printers, servers): These never DHCP, so they have no binding. Solutions: ip source binding (adds a static row to the snooping binding table) OR arp access-list + ip arp inspection filter (whitelist MAC-IP pairs for DAI specifically). Without one of these, DAI drops the static host's ARPs.

Trust overlap: A port trusted for DHCP snooping should usually also be trusted for DAI (both apply to uplinks toward trusted infrastructure).

hack.memory: DAI = child. DHCP snooping = parent. Kill the parent and the child starves. Order: snooping ON → let clients lease → bindings populate → DAI ON. Skip any step and you'll break connectivity for legit users.
hack.practice: Deliberately misconfigure: enable DAI on a VLAN BEFORE enabling DHCP snooping. Clients can't ping the gateway. Check 'show ip arp inspection statistics' — massive drop count. Now enable snooping first, wait for leases, verify bindings, re-test — connectivity returns. Feel the dependency.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The exam loves this dependency. Question: 'You enabled DAI and users lost connectivity — why?' Answer: DHCP snooping not running, so no bindings exist.

5.7.e.info:
ip arp inspection vlan [vlan-list] activates DAI on specific VLANs. Until this command runs, ARP packets pass through the switch untouched, even if snooping is building bindings.

Syntax examples:

Switch(config)# ip arp inspection vlan 10
Switch(config)# ip arp inspection vlan 10,20,30
Switch(config)# ip arp inspection vlan 100-200

Scope: The command applies DAI only to ARP packets riding on the listed VLANs. Other VLANs are unaffected.

Per-VLAN validation options:

ip arp inspection validate src-mac
ip arp inspection validate dst-mac
ip arp inspection validate ip

These are global commands that tighten the checks across all DAI-enabled VLANs.

Per-VLAN logging tuning:

ip arp inspection vlan 10 logging acl-match matchlog
ip arp inspection log-buffer entries 1024
ip arp inspection log-buffer logs 100 interval 10

These control how many violation events are buffered and how often they're reported.

Rate limiting: DAI rate-limits ARP packets per-port by default (15 pps on untrusted). Exceed the limit → port err-disables. Adjust with ip arp inspection limit rate [pps] per interface.

Verify: show ip arp inspection shows enabled VLANs, validation options, and interface trust states. show ip arp inspection statistics displays forwarded/dropped counters.

nodes: Match → forward / No match → drop + log

hack.memory: DAI per-VLAN enable mirrors DHCP snooping's per-VLAN enable. 'ip arp inspection vlan 10' arms enforcement. Don't forget rate limit default is 15 pps — busy environments may need tuning.
hack.practice: Enable DAI on VLAN 10 only. From a client on VLAN 10, send legit traffic → works. From a client on VLAN 20, send traffic → works (DAI not enabled). Now extend to VLAN 20 with 'ip arp inspection vlan 20'. Test again. This maps the scope of the command clearly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam basics: per-VLAN enable command, rate limit default (15 pps), err-disable on overflow.

5.7.e.info:
ip arp inspection trust is the interface-level command that flags a port as trusted for DAI. Trusted ports bypass ARP inspection entirely — every ARP is forwarded without any binding-table check.

Where to apply:

  • Trunk ports between switches — ARPs transit multiple switches and may not match bindings on the current hop
  • Uplinks to distribution/core — same reason
  • Router-facing ports — routers send ARPs for their own interfaces which may not be DHCP-learned
  • Server-facing ports with static IPs (alternatively use static bindings instead of trust)

Where NOT to apply: End-user access ports. Trusting a user port defeats the entire point of DAI.

Interaction with DHCP snooping trust: Trust states for snooping and DAI are independent — you configure them separately. In practice, a port trusted for snooping is usually also trusted for DAI, but you still need both commands:

interface Gi0/24
 ip dhcp snooping trust
 ip arp inspection trust

Default: Every port is untrusted for DAI when DAI is enabled on its VLAN. No explicit trust command = ARP inspection enforced.

Verify: show ip arp inspection interfaces lists all interfaces and their trust state, plus the rate limit in effect.

hack.memory: Same trust rule as DHCP snooping: uplinks trusted, user ports untrusted. DAI trust is a SEPARATE command from snooping trust — you type 'ip dhcp snooping trust' AND 'ip arp inspection trust' on the same uplink. Don't forget one.
hack.practice: On a lab access switch with a trunk to distribution: apply both 'ip dhcp snooping trust' and 'ip arp inspection trust' on the trunk port. Apply neither on user ports. 'show ip arp inspection interfaces' confirms the split. Traffic works. Now remove the DAI trust from the trunk — a flood of ARPs gets dropped (trunk carries ARPs for many hosts that aren't learned on this switch's binding table).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The two trust commands are separate. The exam can ask 'which command trusts a port for DAI?' = 'ip arp inspection trust' (NOT 'ip dhcp snooping trust').

5.7.f.info:
The Layer 2 security features stack in a strict dependency order. Each feature's data comes from the one below it:

  1. DHCP snooping — observes DHCP transactions, builds the binding table, filters server messages on untrusted ports
  2. DAI — reads the binding table to validate ARP packets
  3. IP Source Guard — reads the binding table to validate source IP (and optionally MAC) on every packet

Visual of the chain:

DHCP client requests IP
        ↓
DHCP snooping observes lease
        ↓
Binding table row created  ← DATA SOURCE
   ↓         ↓
  DAI     IP Source Guard
 (ARPs)   (IP packets)

Why order matters: Enable them in this sequence only:

  1. DHCP snooping (global + per-VLAN + trust uplinks)
  2. Wait for bindings to populate (show ip dhcp snooping binding)
  3. DAI (per-VLAN + trust uplinks)
  4. IP Source Guard (per-interface, ip verify source)

If you skip snooping: DAI drops every ARP. IPSG drops every IP packet. The network appears broken.

If you enable DAI before bindings exist: Legit clients can't resolve ARP during the interim and lose connectivity. Always populate first.

Static exceptions needed at every layer: Static-IP devices need ip source binding (creates a binding row) or per-feature ACLs (arp access-list for DAI). Missing these causes persistent drops of legitimate traffic from printers, servers, VoIP phones with static configs.

hack.memory: Three-layer stack: SNOOPING feeds the binding table → DAI and IPSG both READ that table. Enable in order: snooping, wait, DAI, IPSG. Skip a step and downstream features starve. Static hosts need 'ip source binding' at every layer or they get dropped.
hack.practice: Build the full chain on a lab switch and enable in correct order. Verify bindings after step 1. Test ARP after step 3 (DAI). Test IP forwarding after step 4 (IPSG). Then tear it down in the WRONG order — remove snooping first — and watch DAI/IPSG break. This mental model is essential.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam keystone: know the chain and enable order. Snooping is the foundation; DAI and IPSG are consumers of its binding table.

5.7.f.info:
The uplink toward the legitimate DHCP server (and toward the rest of the trusted network) should be trusted for BOTH DHCP snooping AND DAI. These are two separate commands on the same interface — forgetting one breaks the feature that's missing trust.

Correct uplink configuration:

interface GigabitEthernet0/24
 description Uplink to Distribution
 switchport mode trunk
 ip dhcp snooping trust
 ip arp inspection trust

What breaks when you forget one:

  • Missing ip dhcp snooping trust: DHCP Offer/ACK from the real server is dropped at this switch. Clients never get IPs. Binding table stays empty.
  • Missing ip arp inspection trust: ARPs transiting the trunk get dropped because their MAC-IP bindings were learned on a different switch's binding table. Users lose connectivity to destinations behind the trunk.

Why they're independent commands: Cisco kept them separate so you can have asymmetric trust — e.g., trust DHCP but not ARP on a specific port. In practice, this is rare. For uplinks, always configure both.

IP Source Guard trust: IPSG doesn't use a 'trust' concept — instead, you simply don't enable ip verify source on ports where you don't want IPSG (typically trunks, uplinks, server ports). IPSG is opt-in per port.

Verify: show ip dhcp snooping (trusted ports for snooping) + show ip arp inspection interfaces (trusted ports for DAI). Both should list the same uplinks.

hack.memory: Two features, two trust commands, one physical port. If you trust snooping but not DAI (or vice versa), half the features work and half break. Rule: every uplink gets BOTH trust commands — no exceptions unless you have a specific reason.
hack.practice: Type both commands from memory on a trunk port. Shut down first, enable them, no-shut. Verify with both show commands. Now REMOVE only 'ip arp inspection trust' from the trunk — note ARP drops start appearing in 'show ip arp inspection statistics'. Add it back and drops stop. Do the same experiment with snooping trust.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam clarity: snooping trust and DAI trust are separate commands, both needed on uplinks.

5.7.f.info:
The comprehensive Layer 2 access-edge security stack combines four features working in concert. Each addresses a different attack vector, and together they cover the most common LAN threats.

The four layers:

  1. Port security — limits MAC addresses per port, blocks MAC flooding and unauthorized devices
  2. DHCP snooping — filters rogue DHCP servers, builds binding table
  3. DAI — validates ARPs, blocks ARP spoofing / MITM
  4. IP Source Guard — validates source IP, blocks IP spoofing

Attack → Defense mapping:

  • CAM overflow (MAC flood): Port security
  • Rogue DHCP server: DHCP snooping
  • DHCP starvation: Port security + DHCP snooping rate limit
  • ARP spoofing / MITM: DAI
  • IP spoofing: IP Source Guard
  • BPDU injection (rogue switch): BPDU guard
  • Root bridge hijack: Root guard
  • Broadcast/multicast storms: Storm control

Deployment template for every access port:

interface GigabitEthernet0/5
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security violation shutdown
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky
 spanning-tree portfast
 spanning-tree bpduguard enable
 ip verify source

Plus global: DHCP snooping + DAI enabled on VLAN 10, uplinks trusted for both.

Operational note: Deploy in order (port security → snooping → DAI → IPSG) and verify each layer before adding the next. Never enable all four at once on production — you will hit unexpected drops from overlooked static hosts.

hack.memory: Four-layer access-port security: Port security + DHCP snooping + DAI + IPSG. Each blocks a different attack. Plus STP helpers: BPDU guard (rogue switch), Root guard (STP hijack), Storm control (flood). Complete template = pro-grade access layer.
hack.practice: On a lab switch, apply the full template to every access port. Then walk through each attack: plug in extra laptop (port security shutdowns), rogue DHCP (snooping drops Offer), ARP spoof (DAI drops), IP spoof (IPSG drops), rogue switch sending BPDU (BPDU guard shutdowns). One stack, five attacks, five defenses working in series. This is the full picture.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The exam may ask you to match attack → feature. Own this mapping cold: port security=flood, snooping=rogue DHCP, DAI=ARP, IPSG=IP, BPDU guard=rogue switch, root guard=STP.

5.7.g.info:
Port security is a Layer 2 feature that restricts which and how many source MAC addresses a switch port will accept. Once enabled, the switch tracks the MACs seen on the port and takes action if an unauthorized or excess MAC appears.

What it protects against:

  • Unauthorized device attachment — plug in a laptop at a conference-room port and port security drops it.
  • CAM-table overflow (MAC flooding): An attacker floods random source MACs to fill the switch's CAM table. Once full, the switch falls back to flooding unknown unicast out every port — essentially turning it into a hub. Port security caps MACs per port, preventing the flood.
  • Policy enforcement: One-device-per-port rules; per-port MAC whitelists.

Constraints:

  • Port must be in access mode (or explicitly trunk, but CCNA focuses on access). switchport mode access is a prerequisite.
  • Does NOT work on dynamic (auto/desirable) ports — you must pin the port mode first.
  • Not recommended on uplinks, trunks, or server ports (which legitimately carry many MACs).

High-level flow: set access mode → enable port security → set max MACs → set violation mode → optionally specify allowed MACs (static or sticky).

hack.memory: Port security = bouncer at a single door checking MAC IDs. Too many strangers = door slams shut (err-disable). Kills MAC flooding, unauthorized devices, CAM overflow attacks. Prerequisite: access mode.
hack.practice: Set up a lab with a CAM-overflow tool (macof). Watch a fresh switch's CAM table fill in seconds. Now enable port security on the attacker's port with max 2. Flood again — port err-disables before the CAM fills. Feel the before/after.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Port security + violation modes + sticky MAC are one exam unit. Study them together.

5.7.g.info:
Port security's maximum MAC count defines the upper limit of distinct source MAC addresses the port will accept. Exceed the limit → violation fires.

Configuration:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport mode access
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2

Defaults: when port security is enabled without specifying a maximum, the default is 1 MAC.

Typical values:

  • 1 — strict one-device-per-port (most secure, breaks voice/data scenarios)
  • 2 — a PC daisy-chained behind a VoIP phone (phone + PC both need a MAC) — the most common real-world value
  • 3–5 — small hub or unmanaged switch connected to the port (not recommended)

Voice VLAN subtlety: On a port with an access VLAN for data and a voice VLAN for phones, the voice device adds one more MAC. You usually need max 2 to accommodate phone + PC. Some platforms auto-adjust when switchport voice vlan is set.

When the max is hit: The next different source MAC is treated as a violation — action depends on violation mode (protect/restrict/shutdown, default shutdown).

Verify: show port-security interface Gi0/5 shows max, current count, total violation count, and violation mode. show port-security address lists every learned MAC and its port.

hack.memory: Default max = 1. Voice + data port = 2 (phone + PC). Anything higher needs a reason. 'switchport port-security maximum N' sets the cap. Violate the cap and the violation mode decides what happens.
hack.practice: Configure max 2 on a lab access port. Connect a PC — count=1. Connect a second device via hub — count=2. Connect a third — violation (port err-disables if mode=shutdown). 'show port-security interface' shows the progression.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam fact: default max = 1. Voice scenarios need max 2. Know the command: 'switchport port-security maximum [N]'.

5.7.g.info:
Port security's two primary purposes:

  1. Prevent MAC flooding / CAM overflow attacks. Without port security, an attacker can flood a port with thousands of frames using random source MACs. The switch stores each new MAC in its CAM table. When the CAM fills (typically 8k–32k entries), the switch can no longer learn new MACs — it falls back to unknown-unicast flooding, sending every frame out every port in the VLAN. The attacker now captures all traffic on the segment.
  2. Enforce one-device-per-port policy. Many organizations require each network port to be used by a single authorized device. Port security prevents users from attaching a personal switch or hub to multiply access, and prevents unauthorized laptops from connecting in conference rooms, lobbies, or abandoned cubes.

Secondary benefits:

  • Slows lateral movement during a breach — a compromised endpoint can't easily impersonate other MACs.
  • Detects rogue devices early — violations fire syslog/SNMP traps that security teams can alert on.
  • Contributes to compliance (PCI-DSS requires access controls at the port level).

Limitations: Port security alone is not sufficient. A determined attacker can clone the MAC of an authorized device. Combine with 802.1X for real identity-based access control. Port security is the floor, not the ceiling.

hack.memory: Two jobs: (1) stop MAC flooding (CAM overflow → hub mode → attacker sniffs all traffic). (2) enforce one-device-per-port (no rogue laptops). Port security is the FLOOR — combine with 802.1X for real security.
hack.practice: Describe the MAC flooding attack in your own words: attacker generates fake MACs → CAM fills → switch floods all traffic → attacker sees everything. Then describe how max=1 stops this: attacker's second MAC violates the limit before the CAM can fill. Teaching it back locks it in.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The exam scenario: 'attacker sends random MACs until the switch floods all traffic. Which feature prevents this?' = Port security.

5.7.h.info:
Port security has one non-negotiable prerequisite: the port must be set to a non-dynamic mode. The command switchport mode access is the standard path on CCNA — it pins the port to access mode so port security can engage.

Why this matters: Dynamic ports (dynamic auto / dynamic desirable) negotiate their mode via DTP. Port security needs a deterministic mode to know what traffic pattern is normal. On a dynamic port that just negotiated to trunk, enabling port security will fail or behave unpredictably.

Correct sequence:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport mode access     ← STEP 1: pin the mode
 switchport port-security    ← STEP 2: enable the feature

Also valid for specific use cases: switchport mode trunk + port security on a trunk (uncommon, and not tested on CCNA). The access-mode prerequisite is what the exam expects.

Common mistake: Skipping step 1. Typing switchport port-security on a default (dynamic auto) port — the IOS accepts the command but the feature may not activate until the port's mode is resolved. Worse, if DTP later negotiates the port to trunk, port security can behave inconsistently.

Best practice for every access port:

interface Gi0/1 - 48
 switchport mode access
 switchport nonegotiate     ← disables DTP
 switchport port-security

Verify: show interfaces Gi0/5 switchport — look for 'Administrative Mode: static access' before enabling port security.

hack.memory: RULE: 'switchport mode access' FIRST, then 'switchport port-security'. Port security hates dynamic ports. Skip the prereq and the feature doesn't engage cleanly.
hack.practice: On a default port, type 'switchport port-security' without the access-mode line. Check 'show port-security interface' — port may show the config but not be fully enabled. Now add 'switchport mode access' first, re-verify. This teaches the ordering.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam expects you to configure access mode first. Most sim questions include this step — if you skip it, other port security commands fail.

5.7.h.info:
switchport port-security (interface-level) is the master enable for port security on a specific port. It turns on tracking of source MACs and activates the configured maximum, violation mode, and address-learning method.

Syntax:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport mode access
 switchport port-security

What enabling does without any other options:

  • Maximum MACs: 1 (default)
  • Violation mode: shutdown (default) — port err-disables on violation
  • Learning mode: dynamic — first MAC observed is remembered (lost on port bounce or reboot)

So a bare switchport port-security on an access port = one MAC allowed, shutdown on violation, no persistence. This is usable but not ideal — most deployments tune max and use sticky learning.

Disable: no switchport port-security turns off the feature and clears all secured MACs on the port.

Verification:

Switch# show port-security interface Gi0/5
Port Security              : Enabled
Port Status                : Secure-up
Violation Mode             : Shutdown
Aging Time                 : 0 mins
Aging Type                 : Absolute
SecureStatic Address Aging : Disabled
Maximum MAC Addresses      : 1
Total MAC Addresses        : 1
Configured MAC Addresses   : 0
Sticky MAC Addresses       : 0
Last Source Address:Vlan   : aaaa.bbbb.cccc:10
Security Violation Count   : 0

hack.memory: 'switchport port-security' (no other args) = master ON. Defaults kick in: max 1, violation shutdown, dynamic learning. To do anything smarter, add more commands.
hack.practice: Apply just 'switchport mode access' + 'switchport port-security' to a lab port. Connect one device — works. Connect a second — err-disable. Recover with shut/no-shut. Understand what the bare enable does before adding options.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. The exam wants you to know defaults: max 1, violation shutdown. Memorize both.

5.7.h.info:
Two interface-level commands tune how many and which MACs are allowed.

1. Maximum:

switchport port-security maximum [N]

Sets the cap on distinct MACs the port will learn. Default 1. Range typically 1–132 on most platforms (platform-dependent).

2. Static MAC:

switchport port-security mac-address aaaa.bbbb.cccc

Manually whitelists a specific MAC. The MAC is saved in running-config as a permanent static secure entry. Only this MAC (and any others you add) can use the port.

3. Sticky MAC:

switchport port-security mac-address sticky

Auto-learns MACs and writes them into running-config as secure entries (marked 'SecureSticky'). Convenient middle ground between manual and dynamic.

Combining them:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky
 switchport port-security mac-address aaaa.bbbb.cccc     ← static for a known device

With maximum 2: one sticky-learned MAC + one static MAC can coexist.

Interaction with violation: If you configure a static MAC AND the port sees a different MAC, the different MAC is a violation (regardless of whether the max is hit) — because the static MAC has already been 'claimed.'

Verify: show port-security address — lists all secured MACs with type (SecureConfigured / SecureSticky / SecureDynamic) and their associated ports.

right.label: Sticky
items: Lost on reboot without 'copy run start'

hack.memory: Three ways to fill the MAC slots: dynamic (volatile), sticky (learned + saved to running-config), static (typed by admin). Max + mac-address options work together — max 2 + one static MAC = one slot left for a sticky/dynamic learn.
hack.practice: Configure max 2, add one static MAC, enable sticky. Connect a device with a DIFFERENT MAC — sticky learns it into slot 2. 'show port-security address' shows both rows. Connect a third device — violation. Perfect demo of combining modes.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam command syntax: 'switchport port-security maximum N' and 'switchport port-security mac-address [H.H.H | sticky]'.

5.7.h.info:
The canonical port security configuration order on an access port:

  1. Pin the mode: switchport mode access (prerequisite)
  2. Enable port security: switchport port-security
  3. Set the max: switchport port-security maximum [N] (default 1)
  4. Choose MAC learning: switchport port-security mac-address sticky OR switchport port-security mac-address [H.H.H] (static)
  5. Set violation mode: switchport port-security violation [protect|restrict|shutdown] (default shutdown)

Full template:

interface GigabitEthernet0/5
 description User Access Port
 switchport mode access
 switchport access vlan 10
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky
 switchport port-security violation restrict

Order gotchas:

  • You can enter commands in almost any order — IOS accepts them — but port security only activates cleanly when mode=access is in place. If you forget step 1 and type port-security commands, some will fail silently or produce inconsistent behavior.
  • If a port is already up with traffic and you apply port security, the first MAC seen becomes secured (sticky or dynamic). You may get an unexpected MAC locked in.
  • Always shutdown the port, configure, then no shutdown — cleanest approach.

Save sticky MACs: copy running-config startup-config. Sticky MACs live in running-config; without saving, they're lost on reboot and re-learned from scratch.

hack.memory: Five-step recipe: access mode → enable → max → MAC learning → violation. Shortcut mnemonic: 'AM-P-M-M-V' (Access Mode, Port-security, Max, MAC-address, Violation). Save running-config or sticky MACs die on reboot.
hack.practice: Type the full 5-step template from memory, on a lab port, three times in a row. Test it works. Erase and do it again. Muscle memory is what the exam sim rewards.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Expect a simulator question requiring exact port-security config. Know all 5 steps cold.

5.7.i.info:
switchport port-security mac-address sticky tells the switch: automatically learn MACs on this port AND write them into running-config as static secure entries. It's the 'set-and-forget' path between fully manual and fully dynamic MAC learning.

What happens on first connection: A PC plugs in and sends its first frame. The switch learns the source MAC and immediately writes a line into running-config:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky aaaa.bbbb.cccc

That MAC is now a permanent secure entry for as long as it stays in running-config.

Key behaviors:

  • Survives port bounce / link flap — the MAC is in running-config, so interface down/up doesn't lose it
  • Survives clear mac address-table — secure entries aren't touched by that command
  • Does NOT survive reboot unless you save config (copy running-config startup-config or write memory)
  • Stops learning when max is reached — subsequent new MACs trigger violations

When sticky is wrong: Shared ports where multiple devices may legitimately connect over time (conference rooms, hot-desks). Sticky locks in whoever plugged in first.

Remove a sticky MAC:

interface Gi0/5
 no switchport port-security mac-address sticky aaaa.bbbb.cccc

Convert all sticky MACs on an interface to static:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport port-security mac-address sticky

(Once learned, sticky entries appear in running-config exactly like static ones.)

states: Permanent until removed or max reached

hack.memory: Sticky = 'auto-learn, but save it.' First MAC sticks to running-config forever (until you remove it). Skip manual MAC entry, gain persistence. Must 'copy run start' to survive reboot.
hack.practice: Enable sticky on a lab port. Connect a device. Run 'show running-config interface Gi0/5' — see the auto-added 'switchport port-security mac-address sticky XXXX.XXXX.XXXX' line. Bounce the port — MAC stays. Reload without saving — MAC gone. Save, reload — MAC survives.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Sticky is the most-asked port-security learning mode on the exam. Know: auto-learn → running-config → must save to survive reboot.

5.7.i.info:
The gotcha: Sticky MACs live in running-config, not startup-config. If the switch reboots before you save, all learned sticky MACs are lost. The port becomes empty of secure entries, and on the next connection, a fresh MAC is learned and stored. This is fine for the same device — but for a replaced device (e.g., refurbished laptop), the new MAC is learned and 'trusted' automatically.

Reboot scenarios:

  1. Saved config: Sticky MACs survive. Port behaves identically after reboot.
  2. Unsaved config + same device reconnects first: Same MAC is re-learned into an empty sticky slot. Port behaves identically. No alert.
  3. Unsaved config + different device connects first: New MAC is learned into the empty slot. The formerly secure MAC is now rejected if it reconnects. Confusing failures.

Standard fix: After any sticky MAC is learned (typically during initial deployment), run copy running-config startup-config or write memory. Better: put this in your port security deployment checklist so it happens automatically.

Why Cisco didn't make it automatic: Writing every learned MAC immediately to startup-config would cause excessive NVRAM wear and could slow deployment. Sticky is a compromise between persistence and flash-lifetime.

Automation trick: Use EEM or a cron job that periodically runs write memory on switches with lots of sticky learning. Many orgs do this every few hours.

Verify: show port-security address shows 'SecureSticky' vs 'SecureConfigured'. Check show running-config vs show startup-config to confirm whether sticky MACs are saved.

hack.memory: Sticky MACs = running-config only. Reboot without 'copy run start' = all sticky MACs gone. The first device to reconnect gets re-learned — which can be the WRONG device. Always save after sticky learning.
hack.practice: Learn a sticky MAC on a lab port. Verify in 'show run'. Reload without saving — sticky MAC is gone. Connect a DIFFERENT device — its MAC is learned fresh. Now re-plug the original device — violation. This burns the lesson in.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam fact: sticky is in running-config, NOT startup-config. Must save or lose on reboot. This distinction is tested.

5.7.i.info:
Three learning modes for port security MACs — choose based on environment:

ModeHow MAC Gets InIn Running-Config?Survives Port Bounce?Survives Reboot?
DynamicAuto-learnedNoNoNo
StickyAuto-learnedYesYesOnly if saved
StaticManually typedYesYesOnly if saved

When to use each:

  • Dynamic — low-security environments, lab setups, or as a temporary baseline. Rarely used in production because no persistence.
  • Sticky — production workhorse. Admins don't have to track MACs manually; the switch learns each user's device automatically and locks it in. Dominant real-world mode.
  • Static — high-security ports where the allowed MAC is known in advance (e.g., a specific server or printer). Admin explicitly configures the MAC — no auto-learning happens.

Mixed deployment: You can combine static + sticky. Example: max 2, one static MAC for a known printer, sticky for whatever user device plugs in.

switchport port-security maximum 2
switchport port-security mac-address aaaa.bbbb.cccc     ← static printer
switchport port-security mac-address sticky              ← learn user device

Exam distillation: Static = manually typed. Sticky = auto-learned + saved to running-config. Dynamic = auto-learned + NOT saved.

hack.memory: Dynamic = volatile. Sticky = auto + saved. Static = manual + saved. Sticky is the production default. Static is for known MACs like servers. Dynamic is rare.
hack.practice: Apply all three modes on three different lab ports. Connect devices to each. 'show port-security address' shows SecureDynamic / SecureSticky / SecureConfigured — three different types. Reboot each — watch which survives. This table snaps into place quickly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Know the three types and their persistence behavior. This is pure recall on the exam.

5.7.j.info:
Protect is the quietest of the three port security violation modes. When a violation occurs (unauthorized MAC or max exceeded), the switch silently drops the offending frames from the unknown MAC. The port stays up, legitimate traffic continues, and nothing is logged.

Behavior summary:

  • Drop unauthorized MAC's frames: YES
  • Port stays up: YES
  • Syslog message: NO
  • SNMP trap: NO
  • Violation counter increments: NO

Configuration:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport port-security violation protect

Why use protect:

  • Low-disruption enforcement — users see 'network not working' from their unauthorized device but don't know why. No noisy logs from known-bad scenarios (e.g., conference rooms with frequent device changes).
  • Cleanest for automated systems — no syslog flood to ship/index.

Downsides:

  • No visibility — security team has no record of violation attempts. Attackers can probe ports silently.
  • Hard to troubleshoot — if a legit user complains their device doesn't work, you have no log to correlate.

Verification: show port-security interface shows 'Violation Mode : Protect' and 'Security Violation Count : 0' (always 0 in protect mode, even if violations occurred).

hack.memory: PROTECT = silent drop. Frames from unauthorized MAC get dropped; port stays up; NOTHING is logged. Weakest enforcement — attacker probes without leaving evidence. Use only when you want invisible enforcement.
hack.practice: Set protect mode, trigger a violation (second MAC on a max=1 port). Legitimate traffic continues. Run 'show port-security interface' — violation count stays at 0. Check syslog — no message. Compare against restrict mode later.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Key exam fact: Protect = NO log, NO counter. This is what distinguishes it from Restrict.

5.7.j.info:
Restrict is the middle ground. Unauthorized MAC frames are dropped, but the switch also logs the violation (syslog + SNMP trap + counter increment). The port stays up and forwards legitimate traffic from authorized MACs.

Behavior summary:

  • Drop unauthorized MAC's frames: YES
  • Port stays up: YES
  • Syslog message: YES
  • SNMP trap: YES
  • Violation counter increments: YES

Configuration:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport port-security violation restrict

Why use restrict:

  • Visibility without disruption — security team sees every violation attempt in syslog/SIEM, but the port stays operational for legit users. Ideal for environments where err-disable is too aggressive but you need auditing.
  • Troubleshooting data — when a user's device doesn't work, the violation log immediately shows which MAC was rejected.

Syslog example:

%PORT_SECURITY-2-PSECURE_VIOLATION: Security violation occurred, caused by
MAC address 1111.2222.3333 on port GigabitEthernet0/5.

Violation counter:

Switch# show port-security interface Gi0/5
...
Security Violation Count   : 47

When restrict isn't enough: If you need the port to physically stop traffic (HIPAA-sensitive environment, regulatory compliance), use shutdown instead.

hack.memory: RESTRICT = drop + REPORT. Unauthorized MAC dropped, but syslog fires, SNMP trap sent, violation counter climbs. Port stays operational for authorized users. Middle of the three modes — visible but non-disruptive.
hack.practice: Set restrict. Trigger violations. Verify (1) unauthorized traffic drops, (2) authorized traffic flows, (3) 'show port-security interface' violation count increments, (4) syslog has the %PORT_SECURITY-2-PSECURE_VIOLATION message. Compare directly with protect mode (same drop, no log).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam distinction: Restrict increments the violation counter and logs. Protect does neither. Shutdown does both AND err-disables.

5.7.j.info:
Shutdown is port security's default and most aggressive violation mode. On the first violation, the port is immediately placed in err-disabled state, stopping ALL traffic (including from authorized MACs). Syslog logs the event, SNMP fires, and the violation counter increments.

Behavior summary:

  • Drop unauthorized frames: YES (and all other traffic too)
  • Port stays up: NO — goes err-disabled
  • Syslog message: YES
  • SNMP trap: YES
  • Violation counter increments: YES

Configuration:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport port-security violation shutdown

(This is also the default — you get shutdown mode by just enabling port security with no violation command.)

Err-disable state visual: show interfaces Gi0/5 status shows 'err-disabled'. Port LED is amber. show port-security interface shows 'Port Status: Secure-shutdown'.

Recovery options:

  • Manual: interface Gi0/5 → shutdown → no shutdown (must do BOTH — no-shutdown alone doesn't recover from err-disable)
  • Automatic: errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation + errdisable recovery interval 300 (port auto-recovers after 5 minutes)

When to use shutdown: Highest-security environments — financial networks, sensitive research, regulatory compliance. The disruption is the point: unauthorized access means everyone on that port loses service, which gets attention fast.

Why it's the default: Cisco chose the most conservative mode as default. Administrators who want less disruption must explicitly opt into protect or restrict.

hack.memory: SHUTDOWN = slam the whole port. One unauthorized MAC = port goes err-disabled = NOBODY can use it until you recover. Default mode. Most secure, most disruptive. Recover with 'shut / no shut' or auto via errdisable recovery.
hack.practice: Leave violation at default (shutdown). Trigger one violation — port goes err-disabled immediately. 'show interfaces status' shows err-disabled. Recover manually (shut / no-shut). Then configure 'errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation' with interval 30 — trigger again, wait 30s, watch auto-recovery.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Shutdown = default. Memorize this fact. Also: err-disable recovery syntax ('errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation').

5.7.j.info:
switchport port-security violation [protect | restrict | shutdown] — interface-level command that selects which action occurs when a port security violation is detected.

Three options, increasing severity:

ModeDrops FramesLogsCountsShuts Port
ProtectYesNoNoNo
RestrictYesYesYesNo
Shutdown (default)YesYesYesYes (err-disable)

Default: If you don't specify violation mode, shutdown is used.

Example:

interface Gi0/5
 switchport mode access
 switchport port-security
 switchport port-security maximum 2
 switchport port-security violation restrict

Choosing the right mode:

  • Protect — low-risk environments, you want the feature invisible.
  • Restrict — you want visibility without disruption. Good for moderately secure corporate networks.
  • Shutdown — default. Use when disruption on violation is acceptable or desired.

Exam recall trick: alphabetical order matches severity: Protect (least) → Restrict (middle) → Shutdown (most).

Change mode without losing config: Just re-issue the violation command with a new mode. No shutdown/no-shutdown needed.

hack.memory: P-R-S, alphabetical = severity order. Protect = silent. Restrict = drop + log. Shutdown = err-disable (DEFAULT). The command: 'switchport port-security violation [mode]'. Default is shutdown — you don't have to type it to get it.
hack.practice: On three lab ports, apply each of the three violation modes. Trigger a violation on each and compare: port 1 (protect) — nothing visible. Port 2 (restrict) — log message + counter. Port 3 (shutdown) — err-disabled. Document the differences.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Classic exam question: 'which violation mode drops traffic but keeps the port up and logs?' = Restrict. Know the table cold.

5.7.k.info:
When a port enters err-disabled state (from port security, BPDU guard, DHCP rate limit, etc.), it stops forwarding all traffic. The simplest recovery is manual: shutdown and no-shutdown the interface.

The recovery sequence:

Switch# configure terminal
Switch(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/5
Switch(config-if)# shutdown
Switch(config-if)# no shutdown

Why BOTH commands: no shutdown alone does NOT bring an err-disabled port back up. The port must be administratively shutdown first (cleanly setting its state to admin-down), then re-enabled with no-shutdown. This cycles the state machine back to a clean 'up' state.

Before recovering — INVESTIGATE:

  1. Find the cause: show interfaces GigabitEthernet0/5 shows 'err-disabled' and often the reason (e.g., 'psecure-violation')
  2. For port security: show port-security interface Gi0/5 shows which MAC triggered the violation
  3. Address the underlying problem (rogue device, misconfig, cable issue) before recovering — otherwise the port will err-disable again within minutes

Common mistake: recovering without investigating. The port comes up, violates again, goes back down. Repeat until someone finally checks the logs.

Recovery script trick: For quick recovery during known maintenance:

Switch# clear port-security sticky interface Gi0/5
Switch# configure terminal
Switch(config)# interface Gi0/5
Switch(config-if)# shutdown
Switch(config-if)# no shutdown

(Clearing sticky MACs before recovery lets the port re-learn fresh.)

Verify recovery: show interfaces Gi0/5 status should show 'connected' (not err-disabled). 'show port-security interface' should show 'Secure-up'.

hack.memory: Manual recovery = SHUT then NO SHUT. Both commands. No-shut alone doesn't work on err-disabled ports. Before recovering: find the cause, fix it, THEN recover. Otherwise you'll play whack-a-mole.
hack.practice: Trigger err-disable via port security violation. Try 'no shutdown' alone — port stays err-disabled. Now issue 'shutdown' then 'no shutdown' — port recovers. This teaches the two-command requirement.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam question: 'How do you recover an err-disabled port?' = shutdown followed by no-shutdown. Both commands needed.

5.7.k.info:
errdisable recovery cause [reason] is a global configuration command that enables automatic recovery from err-disabled state for specific causes. When enabled, the switch waits a timer and then auto-recovers the port — no admin intervention.

Syntax:

Switch(config)# errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation
Switch(config)# errdisable recovery cause bpduguard
Switch(config)# errdisable recovery cause dhcp-rate-limit
Switch(config)# errdisable recovery cause arp-inspection

Common causes:

  • psecure-violation — port security violation
  • bpduguard — BPDU guard fired
  • dhcp-rate-limit — DHCP snooping rate limit exceeded
  • arp-inspection — DAI rate limit or drop
  • link-flap — excessive port flapping
  • udld — unidirectional link detection
  • all — enable auto-recovery for ALL causes (convenient but less targeted)

Shortcut: errdisable recovery cause all turns on auto-recovery for everything. Use cautiously — some causes (like root-inconsistent) may warrant permanent shutdown until investigated.

Companion command: errdisable recovery interval [seconds] — sets how long the switch waits before attempting recovery. Default 300 (5 minutes).

Verification: show errdisable recovery shows which causes have auto-recovery enabled and the current interval. Example output:

ErrDisable Reason            Timer Status
-----------------            -------------
psecure-violation            Enabled
bpduguard                    Enabled
arp-inspection               Disabled
...
Timer interval: 300 seconds

Best practice: Enable for transient causes (link-flap, rate-limit) but leave manual for intentional-looking causes (BPDU guard on an access port).

hack.memory: errdisable recovery cause = auto-parole for err-disabled ports. Pick the cause (psecure-violation, bpduguard, dhcp-rate-limit) or use 'all'. Pair with 'errdisable recovery interval N' for timer. 'show errdisable recovery' to verify.
hack.practice: Enable auto-recovery for port security: 'errdisable recovery cause psecure-violation' + 'errdisable recovery interval 30'. Trigger a violation — port goes err-disabled. Wait 30 seconds — port auto-recovers. Compare to manual recovery cycle.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Know the command syntax: 'errdisable recovery cause [reason]'. Common causes tested: psecure-violation, bpduguard, dhcp-rate-limit.

5.7.k.info:
errdisable recovery interval [seconds] sets the timer that the switch waits before attempting to auto-recover an err-disabled port. Applies to all causes that have auto-recovery enabled.

Syntax:

Switch(config)# errdisable recovery interval 300

Range and default:

  • Default: 300 seconds (5 minutes)
  • Range: 30 to 86400 seconds (30 seconds to 24 hours)

Tuning guidance:

  • 30–60 seconds: Lab environments, rapid testing. In production this can cause oscillation if the underlying issue isn't fixed.
  • 300 seconds (default): Good general purpose. Long enough to notice in monitoring, short enough for users to come back soon.
  • 600+ seconds: Production environments where you want repeated violations to stay down longer (forces admin to investigate).

Global setting: The interval applies to ALL enabled causes — you can't have different intervals for psecure-violation vs bpduguard. If you need per-cause timing, it's an operational discipline issue (document which causes should be recovered manually vs auto).

Verification: show errdisable recovery — final line shows 'Timer interval: N seconds'.

When recovery is attempted: At the interval's end, the switch automatically issues a shutdown / no-shutdown sequence on the err-disabled port. If the underlying condition still exists (e.g., rogue MAC still connected), the port err-disables again and the timer restarts.

hack.memory: Interval = timer between err-disable and auto-recover. Default 300s (5 min). Range 30s to 24 hours. ONE timer applies to all causes. 'errdisable recovery interval N'.
hack.practice: Set interval to 30, enable auto-recovery for psecure-violation, trigger a violation. Time the recovery — should be ~30 seconds. Now set interval to 3600, repeat — 1 hour wait. Feel the knob work.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 53. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 30. Exam fact: default interval is 300 seconds. Know the command and the default.

5.8.a.info:
Authentication is the first A in AAA and answers the fundamental question: 'Who are you?' It is the process of proving a claimed identity by presenting credentials (something you know, have, or are) and having them verified against a trusted source.

Credential categories (multi-factor building blocks):

  • Something you know — password, PIN, passphrase
  • Something you have — hardware token (YubiKey, RSA SecurID), smart card, phone (for push/OTP)
  • Something you are — fingerprint, face, iris, voice (biometrics)

Where authentication happens in networking:

  • Device admin — SSH/console/HTTPS login to a router or switch (credentials checked against local user DB, TACACS+, or RADIUS)
  • Network access — 802.1X supplicant presents credentials to the authenticator (switch/AP), which relays them to a RADIUS server
  • VPN — remote user provides username/password or certificate, checked by AAA

Why it's step 1: You cannot authorize actions or account for activity if you don't know who the actor is. Identity is the foundation.

Common Cisco configuration:

aaa new-model
username admin privilege 15 secret StrongPass!
aaa authentication login default group tacacs+ local

This says: enable AAA, create a local admin user, and authenticate login attempts against TACACS+ first, falling back to local users if TACACS+ is unreachable.

hack.memory: Authentication = 'WHO are you?' First A. Three credential factors: know (password), have (token), are (biometric). Identity first, permissions next, logging last. You can't skip this step.
hack.practice: Walk through a VPN login mentally: you type username + password (something you know) + hit a YubiKey (something you have). Two-factor auth. Both credentials verified → you're authenticated. Now the system decides what you can access (authorization).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 50. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 10 + 27. Clearly distinguish authentication (identity) from authorization (permissions) — most AAA exam questions test this distinction.

5.8.a.info:
Authorization is the second A and answers: 'What are you allowed to do?' After authentication has established WHO the user is, authorization determines which resources, commands, VLANs, ACLs, or network segments they may access.

Examples of authorization decisions:

  • Admin user logs into a router → AAA returns privilege 15 (full config access)
  • Helpdesk user logs in → AAA returns privilege 5 with specific show commands unlocked, no config access
  • Employee's laptop authenticates via 802.1X → RADIUS returns VLAN 10 (corporate)
  • Guest's phone authenticates on the same network → RADIUS returns VLAN 100 (guest-only, internet access)
  • Contractor authenticates → RADIUS pushes a downloadable ACL restricting source IPs

Granularity depends on protocol:

  • RADIUS — returns attribute-value pairs at session start (VLAN, ACL, QoS). Cannot authorize individual CLI commands.
  • TACACS+ — supports per-command authorization. Every CLI command typed can be checked against policy before execution. This is why TACACS+ dominates device administration.

Authorization without authentication is meaningless. Every authorization decision is tied to an identity.

Cisco configuration:

aaa authorization exec default group tacacs+ local
aaa authorization commands 15 default group tacacs+ local

These tell the device: when a user tries to enter exec mode or run a privilege-15 command, check TACACS+ first.
