Configuration on Cisco WLC:

  • WLAN Security → Layer 2 → WPA+WPA2 or WPA3
  • Auth Key Mgmt → 802.1X (instead of PSK)
  • AAA Servers → point to RADIUS IP + shared key
  • The WLC then handles EAPoL with clients and RADIUS with the server

Port state mapping (wired ↔ wireless):

  • Wired 802.1X uses physical switch ports with EAPoL
  • Wireless 802.1X uses association state instead of physical ports — 'unauthorized' means associated-but-blocked, 'authorized' means fully forwarding

Exam fact: Wireless authenticator = WLC (or AP). It enforces, doesn't decide. Decision is made by the RADIUS server.

hack.memory: Wireless authenticator = WLC (or AP). Enforces but doesn't decide. Relays EAP to RADIUS, applies the answer. In lightweight Cisco deployments, WLC is the authenticator; APs are just radios.
hack.practice: On a Cisco WLC GUI, configure a WPA2-Enterprise WLAN. AAA servers tab → add RADIUS server. Security → Layer 2 → 802.1X. Save. Associate a supplicant — watch 'Monitor → Clients' show the authentication flow progress. The WLC is playing authenticator live.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Know the wireless authenticator = WLC. In autonomous AP deployments = AP.

5.9.f.info:
The authentication server for wireless 802.1X is a RADIUS server — typically Cisco ISE, Microsoft NPS, FreeRADIUS, or Aruba ClearPass. It validates the user's credentials and decides accept or reject.

What the server does for a wireless auth request:

  1. Receives RADIUS Access-Request from the WLC, containing the EAP-over-RADIUS payload
  2. Selects an EAP method based on server policy (usually PEAP or EAP-TLS)
  3. Runs the EAP method conversation through the WLC to the client:
    • PEAP: sets up TLS tunnel with server cert → inner MSCHAPv2 auth against AD
    • EAP-TLS: mutual cert validation (client cert + server cert)
  4. Validates credentials against identity store (AD, internal DB, CA)
  5. Applies policy — role, location, device health, time of day
  6. Returns Access-Accept with attributes:
    • Tunnel-Type = VLAN
    • Tunnel-Private-Group-ID = VLAN number
    • Filter-Id = named ACL
    • Session-Timeout = re-auth interval
  7. Returns the Master Session Key (MSK) — the WLC uses this to derive session keys for the client

Why Cisco ISE is common:

  • Deep integration with Cisco WLCs and switches
  • Built-in device profiling (knows if the device is a printer, iPhone, Windows laptop)
  • Policy engine supports complex conditions (user + device + location + health)
  • Guest portal and BYOD workflows
  • Posture checks (AV installed, patches current)

Microsoft NPS is common in Windows-only shops because it integrates natively with AD without additional licensing.

FreeRADIUS is popular in academia and service-provider environments — open source, massive scale, flexible scripting via Perl/Python modules.

Exam fact: Wireless auth server = RADIUS server. Cisco ISE is the flagship; know the name.

hack.memory: Wireless auth server = RADIUS = Cisco ISE / Microsoft NPS / FreeRADIUS. The BRAIN. Validates credentials against AD/CA, returns Access-Accept with VLAN/ACL attributes + Master Session Key. The WLC enforces what this server decides.
hack.practice: Install FreeRADIUS on Linux. Add a user. Configure a WLC to point at FreeRADIUS. Authenticate a wireless client. See the FreeRADIUS log: request from WLC → EAP-PEAP conversation → credentials validated → Access-Accept with VLAN attribute returned. You just built the full wireless 802.1X trio.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 56. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Auth server = RADIUS — always. Cisco ISE is the flagship brand name to know.

5.10.a.info:
The primary management interface for a Cisco Wireless LAN Controller is an HTTPS web GUI accessible via a browser. Administrators point their browser at the WLC's management IP address on port 443 and log in with admin credentials.

Access URL: https://[wlc-management-ip] (e.g., https://10.0.0.2)

What the GUI provides:

  • Dashboard — total APs, associated clients, network health, rogue APs
  • WLANs — create/modify/delete WLANs, security profiles, QoS
  • Controller settings — interfaces, ports, NTP, DHCP, general system
  • Wireless — AP management, RF profiles, channel/power, mesh
  • Security — AAA servers, ACLs, rogue AP policies, web authentication
  • Management — SNMP, syslog, software upgrades, admin accounts

Why HTTPS (not HTTP):

  • Encrypts admin credentials and config changes in transit
  • HTTP is usually disabled by default on modern WLCs (or warned against)
  • Most enterprises replace the default self-signed cert with a CA-signed cert to eliminate browser warnings

Certificate management: WLCs ship with a self-signed HTTPS certificate, which triggers browser warnings. Enterprises typically replace this with a certificate from their internal CA so admins can log in without warnings. Configured under Management → HTTP-HTTPS.

Access control:

  • Default admin user is often 'admin' — change on first login
  • Local user database in the Management tab
  • TACACS+ or RADIUS for centralized admin authentication (corporate best practice)
  • Optional management ACLs to restrict which IPs can reach the management interface

Exam expectation: Know that WLC management is HTTPS-based. Browser + management IP = the admin path.

hack.memory: WLC GUI = HTTPS to the management IP. Primary day-to-day admin tool. Major tabs: Monitor, WLANs, Controller, Wireless, Security, Management. Replace self-signed cert with CA-signed in production.
hack.practice: If you have access to Cisco Packet Tracer (has WLC 3504 simulation), Cisco CML, or Cisco DevNet Sandbox, connect to a WLC GUI. Click through each top-level tab and sub-menu. Don't configure yet — just orient yourself to where things live.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam expects you to know HTTPS + management IP for GUI access. The GUI is where all CCNA WLC tasks happen.

5.10.a.info:
When a WLC comes out of the box or is factory-reset, it has no IP configuration — you can't reach it over the network. Initial setup requires the console port to configure management IP, admin credentials, and basic system parameters.

Physical connection:

  • Serial console cable (RJ-45 console or USB-console on newer models) from laptop to WLC
  • Terminal emulator (PuTTY, minicom, screen) at 9600 baud, 8N1

Initial configuration wizard: The WLC runs an interactive script on first boot that prompts for:

  1. System name (hostname)
  2. Admin username and password
  3. Management interface IP, netmask, gateway (this is what you'll use for HTTPS later)
  4. Management VLAN ID
  5. Physical port assignment for management
  6. Virtual interface IP (typically 1.1.1.1 — used internally for DHCP relay and web auth; doesn't need to be routable)
  7. Mobility/RF group name
  8. Country code (regulates channel/power)
  9. Time zone / NTP server
  10. Optional: AP Management interface (or tie to management), initial WLAN settings

After initial setup: Connect the WLC to the network (trunk port on a switch, with the management VLAN), reach the management IP via HTTPS, and continue configuration in the GUI.

Console access going forward: After setup, console is reserved for emergency recovery (lost admin password, network unreachable, boot issues). Day-to-day admin is via HTTPS GUI.

Exam fact: Initial setup = console. Ongoing admin = HTTPS GUI. The console is only for bootstrap and emergency recovery.

hack.memory: No IP on a fresh WLC = no network access = must use CONSOLE. Initial setup wizard walks through hostname, admin creds, management IP, VLAN, virtual IP (1.1.1.1), country code, NTP. After setup, use HTTPS GUI for ongoing admin.
hack.practice: In a lab, reset a WLC to factory defaults. Connect console. Watch the initial setup wizard run. Complete it end-to-end. Then disconnect console, reach management IP via HTTPS, and log in. This is the official bootstrap sequence.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Console setup is a 1-question recall topic. Know: console cable, setup wizard, management IP bootstrap.

5.10.a.info:
WLCs have a CLI accessible via SSH or console, but it's noticeably different from IOS CLI and is used far less than the GUI. Most day-to-day WLC administration happens through the web interface.

Why the WLC CLI is limited:

  • WLCs run AireOS (the traditional Cisco controller OS) — different from IOS
  • Newer Cisco 9800-series WLCs run IOS-XE, which has a more familiar CLI but still discourages line-by-line configuration for wireless-specific tasks
  • Wireless configuration has too many interdependent settings (WLAN, interface, AP group, RF profile, security policy) to configure cleanly via CLI
  • The GUI's wizards and validation catch misconfigurations that CLI typos would silently accept

AireOS CLI commands (legacy 3504, 5508, etc.):

(Cisco Controller) > show sysinfo
(Cisco Controller) > show wlan summary
(Cisco Controller) > show client summary
(Cisco Controller) > config wlan create 1 MyWLAN MySSID
(Cisco Controller) > config wlan security wpa wpa2 ciphers aes enable 1
(Cisco Controller) > config wlan enable 1

9800 IOS-XE CLI (newer controllers):

WLC# show wireless summary
WLC# configure terminal
WLC(config)# wlan MyWLAN 1 MySSID
WLC(config-wlan)# security wpa psk set-key ascii 0 MyPassword
WLC(config-wlan)# no shutdown

When CLI is useful:

  • Bulk automation — scripts, Ansible playbooks, Python netmiko
  • Troubleshooting — some debug output is CLI-only
  • Emergency recovery — GUI unreachable
  • Specific features — a few advanced knobs exist only in CLI

Exam framing: CCNA expects you to configure WLANs via the GUI. CLI knowledge is minimal. If asked where WLAN configuration happens: answer is GUI.

hack.memory: WLC CLI exists but is rarely used. AireOS (old) or IOS-XE (9800 series). Main use: automation, debug, emergency recovery. For CCNA exam purposes, WLAN config = GUI.
hack.practice: If you have access to a WLC, SSH in and run 'show wlan summary', 'show client summary'. Notice the output differs from IOS. Understand that for CCNA, the GUI is the expected interface.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Minor recall: WLC CLI exists, but GUI dominates. Don't memorize CLI commands for CCNA.

5.10.b.info:
Creating a new WLAN on a Cisco WLC requires three identifying pieces of information. All three are mandatory; the GUI prompts for each during WLAN creation.

The three identifiers:

  1. Profile Name — an internal, administrative label used by the WLC to identify this WLAN in configuration, reports, and logs. NOT broadcast to clients. Example: Corporate-WiFi-Employees. Convention: descriptive and unique within the WLC, usually ASCII with hyphens/underscores.
  2. SSID (Service Set Identifier) — the network name that wireless clients see in their Wi-Fi list. This IS broadcast in beacons (unless hidden). Example: CorpNet. Up to 32 characters. Multiple WLANs can share the same SSID (rarely done; usually a 1:1 mapping).
  3. WLAN ID — a unique numeric identifier used internally on the WLC, range 1 to 512 on most platforms. Each WLAN must have a unique ID. Used in reports, AP-group associations, and automation.

Where to enter them (WLC GUI):

  • WLANs tab → top-right menu: Create New → Go
  • Type: WLAN (vs Guest LAN, Remote LAN)
  • Profile Name: [admin-visible label]
  • SSID: [client-visible name]
  • ID: [1-512, auto-increments usually]
  • Apply → WLAN is created in disabled state

After creation: WLAN appears in the WLANs list but is typically disabled. You configure Security, General (interface/VLAN), and Advanced settings, then enable it (5.10.e).

hack.memory: Three WLAN fields: Profile (admin label), SSID (what clients see), ID (1-512 unique number). Mnemonic: PSI — Profile / SSID / ID. All three mandatory. Profile and SSID can be different — they serve different audiences.
hack.practice: In a WLC simulator, create 3 WLANs. For WLAN 1, set profile='Employees' and SSID='CorpNet'. For WLAN 2, profile='Guests' and SSID='Guest-WiFi'. For WLAN 3, profile='IoT-Sensors' and SSID='IoT'. Confirm all three appear in the WLANs tab with different IDs (1, 2, 3).
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam may show a WLC screenshot and ask to identify the three WLAN fields. Know PSI.

5.10.b.info:
The Profile Name and SSID serve different audiences and can be different strings. This is a common exam gotcha.

Profile Name:

  • Audience: administrators
  • Location: WLC GUI, configuration exports, reports, logs
  • Visibility: internal only — clients NEVER see this
  • Purpose: human-readable label for admin operations
  • Convention: descriptive, unique, with hyphens or underscores (no spaces usually)

SSID:

  • Audience: wireless clients
  • Location: 802.11 beacons broadcast over the air
  • Visibility: external — everyone scanning for Wi-Fi sees this
  • Purpose: user-friendly network name
  • Convention: short, brandable (e.g., 'CorpNet', 'Starbucks WiFi', 'SpaceCadets')

Why distinguish them:

  1. Multiple WLANs with the same SSID — e.g., one WLAN profile for AP group A (maps to VLAN 10) and another profile for AP group B (maps to VLAN 20), both broadcasting SSID 'Corp'. Clients see one network name but are placed in different VLANs based on which AP they associated with.
  2. Admin readability — 'Corp-Employees-Floor5-VLAN10' is useful in the GUI but ugly as a client-facing SSID.
  3. SSID rebranding — you can change the SSID without changing the profile name (or vice versa), avoiding risk of breaking references.

Common setup for simple WLANs: profile name = SSID. But know that they're separate fields and can differ.

Exam trap: 'What do wireless clients see when they scan for Wi-Fi?' = SSID (NOT profile name).

hack.memory: Profile = admin label. SSID = client name. Different audiences, can be different strings. Clients see the SSID. Admins see the profile in the GUI. Common to match them for simplicity, but you CAN differ.
hack.practice: In a simulator, deliberately create a WLAN where Profile='Corporate-Employees-VLAN-10' and SSID='CorpNet'. Scan from a client — see 'CorpNet'. Check WLC GUI — see 'Corporate-Employees-VLAN-10'. Two identities, same WLAN.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam may test profile vs SSID distinction — know that clients see SSID, admins see profile.

5.10.b.info:
After you create a WLAN and configure its security, interface, and other settings, it is still disabled by default. It will not broadcast or accept clients until you explicitly enable it via the General tab.

Why disabled by default:

  • Safety against misconfiguration — a brand-new WLAN might have wrong security settings, wrong VLAN mapping, or other issues. Keeping it disabled until ready prevents broadcasting a broken config to the network.
  • Staged rollout — admins often create WLANs during maintenance windows but don't want them visible until a specific go-live time
  • Testing — lets admins verify config before making the WLAN visible

Enable the WLAN:

  1. Navigate to WLANs tab → click the WLAN ID
  2. General tab → Status dropdown → select Enabled
  3. Click Apply at bottom

After enabling:

  • Status column in the WLANs list shows 'Enabled'
  • Associated APs begin broadcasting the SSID in beacons
  • Clients scanning for Wi-Fi see the SSID
  • Authenticated clients can associate and get IPs from the mapped VLAN

The #1 new-WLAN troubleshooting scenario: 'Users can't see the new SSID.' First check: is the WLAN Status = Enabled? This is the most common oversight because all the security and interface config looks correct — the admin just forgot to flip the Enabled switch.

Disable a WLAN: same path, set Status = Disabled, Apply. The SSID immediately stops broadcasting and associated clients are kicked off.

hack.memory: New WLAN = DISABLED by default. You must flip Status = Enabled + Apply. The #1 rookie mistake: perfect config, forgot to enable, users can't see the SSID. If asked 'users can't see the new network,' check Enabled first.
hack.practice: Create a WLAN in a simulator, configure security + interface mapping, leave Status=Disabled. Scan from a client — SSID not visible. Now flip Status=Enabled, Apply. Scan again — SSID appears. Associate, get an IP. Feel the before/after.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Most-tested easy-point question: what's missing when a new SSID isn't visible? Answer: WLAN status not enabled.

5.10.c.info:
For WPA2-Personal on a Cisco WLC, the correct security combination is:

  • Layer 2 Security: WPA+WPA2 (or WPA2 only)
  • WPA2 Policy: Enabled (checkbox checked)
  • WPA2 Encryption: AES (checked)
  • WPA Policy: Disabled (unchecked — legacy WPA/TKIP)
  • Auth Key Management: PSK (checked)
  • PSK Format: ASCII (simpler) or Hex
  • Pre-Shared Key: 8-63 ASCII chars or 64 hex chars

Why this combination:

  • WPA2 (not WPA) — uses AES-CCMP instead of broken TKIP/RC4
  • AES (not TKIP) — strongest encryption, mandatory for modern security
  • PSK — authentication via shared passphrase (Personal mode)

GUI navigation:

  1. WLANs tab → click your WLAN
  2. Security tab → Layer 2 sub-tab
  3. Layer 2 Security dropdown → WPA+WPA2
  4. WPA+WPA2 Parameters → check WPA2 Policy, then check AES under WPA2 Encryption
  5. Auth Key Management → check PSK
  6. Enter PSK → choose ASCII → type passphrase (minimum 8 chars; recommended 16+ with complexity)
  7. Apply

PSK best practices:

  • Minimum 12 characters
  • Mix of upper/lower/numbers/symbols
  • Not a dictionary word
  • Rotate periodically (though in practice rarely done for Personal)

Verification: After Apply, the WLAN's Security column in the WLANs list shows 'WPA2+AES+PSK' or similar.

Exam framing: 'What's the correct security config for WPA2-Personal?' = WPA2 enabled + AES + PSK. 'What encryption should you select?' = AES.

hack.memory: WPA2-Personal recipe: WPA+WPA2 mode → check WPA2 policy → AES encryption → PSK auth → enter passphrase. Mnemonic 'MVCAK': Mode, Version, Cipher, Auth, Key. AES always. TKIP never.
hack.practice: Walk through the Security tab on a WLC simulator. Check WPA+WPA2, enable WPA2, select AES, enable PSK, enter passphrase. Apply. Connect a test client. This is the exact flow the exam expects you to know.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. The most-tested WLC security config on CCNA. Know the 5-step recipe cold.

5.10.c.info:
If a WLC security config shows TKIP enabled, that is the problem the exam is testing you to identify. Modern WPA2 deployments should use AES only — TKIP is deprecated and introduces weaknesses.

Why TKIP is wrong in modern WLC configs:

  • RC4-based — TKIP still uses the weakened RC4 cipher under the hood
  • Beck-Tews attack (2008) — packet injection possible on TKIP networks
  • Performance: TKIP has lower throughput than AES on modern hardware
  • Mandatory AES for 802.11n+ — enabling TKIP forces the radio to drop to 802.11g speeds (54 Mbps) instead of 300+ Mbps with 802.11n/ac/ax + AES
  • Wi-Fi Alliance deprecation — WPA3 certification forbids TKIP entirely

Common exam scenarios:

  • Screenshot shows WPA+WPA2 with both AES and TKIP checked — the fix is to UNCHECK TKIP, leave AES checked
  • Screenshot shows WPA Policy enabled with TKIP, WPA2 not enabled — the fix is to disable WPA, enable WPA2 with AES
  • Mixed WPA/WPA2 'compatibility' config — often uses TKIP for backward compat, which is now unnecessary and harmful

Correct configuration: Always disable TKIP. Enable AES. Disable legacy WPA (unless you have pre-2004 devices that absolutely can't upgrade — extremely rare in 2026).

WPA3 transition mode note: If running WPA2/WPA3 Transition, both WPA2 (AES) and WPA3 (SAE + AES) coexist. Neither uses TKIP. TKIP should never be in a modern config.

Exam takeaway: TKIP = wrong. AES = right. If you see TKIP in a config screenshot, that's the answer to 'what's the security problem?'

hack.memory: TKIP = ALWAYS WRONG in modern WLCs. Uncheck it. Use AES. TKIP forces the radio to 802.11g speeds (54 Mbps) and uses broken RC4. Any screenshot showing TKIP enabled = that's the bug.
hack.practice: In a WLC simulator, intentionally enable TKIP alongside AES. Apply. Now look at the WLANs list — some platforms show a warning. Check the radio's operational rate — 802.11g cap (not 802.11n/ac). This visually demonstrates why TKIP is bad.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam: spot-the-bug question with TKIP enabled = the wrong setting. Know to disable it.

5.10.c.info:
Step-by-step procedure for configuring WPA2-PSK on a Cisco WLC — the exact sequence the CCNA exam expects:

  1. Create the WLAN (if not already done)
    • WLANs tab → Create New → Go
    • Type: WLAN, enter Profile Name, SSID, ID → Apply
  2. Open the WLAN for editing
    • Click the WLAN ID in the WLANs list
  3. Navigate to Security → Layer 2
    • Layer 2 Security dropdown → WPA+WPA2
  4. Configure WPA2 Parameters
    • WPA Policy: UNCHECKED (legacy — don't enable)
    • WPA2 Policy: CHECKED
    • WPA2 Encryption → AES: CHECKED
    • WPA2 Encryption → TKIP: UNCHECKED
  5. Configure Auth Key Management
    • 802.1X: UNCHECKED (this would be Enterprise)
    • CCKM: UNCHECKED (legacy Cisco fast roaming)
    • PSK: CHECKED
    • FT 802.1X / FT PSK: UNCHECKED (advanced fast transition)
  6. Enter the Pre-Shared Key
    • PSK Format: ASCII (simpler) or Hex
    • Enter passphrase (8-63 ASCII chars, or 64 hex chars)
  7. Configure General tab
    • Status: Enabled
    • Interface/Interface Group: select the dynamic interface mapped to the correct VLAN
  8. Click Apply at bottom

Verification:

  • WLANs list shows 'Enabled' status and 'WPA2+AES+PSK' in Security column
  • Associated APs start broadcasting the SSID
  • Test client connects with passphrase
  • Monitor → Clients shows the client as 'Associated' and 'Authenticated'

Exam simulator expectation: You may be asked to walk through this flow in a simulated WLC. Know every checkbox — they're predictable and testable.

hack.memory: Full config walkthrough: WLAN created → Security → Layer 2 → WPA+WPA2 → WPA2 enabled + AES + PSK → passphrase → General → Enabled + Interface → Apply. Every checkbox matters. TKIP unchecked, 802.1X unchecked, PSK checked.
hack.practice: Memorize and execute the 8-step procedure three times in a WLC simulator. Then blindfold yourself (mentally) and narrate the steps out loud. If you can do it without looking, you're ready for the exam sim.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Sim questions ask for exact WLC configuration steps — have the sequence locked in.

5.10.d.info:
Every WLAN must be mapped to a WLC interface, which is in turn associated with a VLAN. This chain determines which wired network segment (IP subnet, DHCP scope, policy domain) wireless clients are placed on when they connect.

The mapping chain:

WLAN (SSID) → WLC Interface (dynamic) → VLAN → Wired Segment (subnet/ACLs/gateway)

WLC interface types:

  • Management interface — used for WLC admin access (HTTPS, SSH) and AP-to-WLC communication (CAPWAP). Usually VLAN 1 or a dedicated mgmt VLAN. NOT typically used for client data.
  • AP-manager interface — on older WLCs, a separate interface for CAPWAP traffic. In newer platforms, often merged with management.
  • Dynamic interface — one per VLAN you want to place clients on. Each has its own IP, VLAN tag, DHCP scope association, and gateway. This is where WLAN→VLAN mapping happens.
  • Virtual interface — internal-only, typically 1.1.1.1. Used by DHCP relay and web authentication. Not mapped to client traffic directly.
  • Service port — dedicated out-of-band management port.

Example mapping:

WLAN (SSID)Dynamic InterfaceVLANSegment
Employeecorp-data1010.0.10.0/24 corporate
Guestguest-net100192.168.100.0/24 internet-only
Voicevoice-vlan5010.0.50.0/24 voice (QoS)
IoTiot-segment20010.0.200.0/24 IoT restricted

Creating a dynamic interface: Controller tab → Interfaces → New → enter name and VLAN ID → configure IP, netmask, gateway, DHCP server → Apply.

Segmentation outcome: Different SSIDs → different VLANs → different security policies. A guest user can't reach corporate resources because they're on VLAN 100 (internet-only), while employees are on VLAN 10 (full corporate).

hack.memory: Chain: WLAN → Dynamic Interface → VLAN → wired segment. Different SSIDs → different VLANs → different security zones. Dynamic interface = the bridge between wireless and wired.
hack.practice: In a WLC simulator, create two dynamic interfaces: 'corp-data' on VLAN 10 and 'guest-net' on VLAN 100. Assign each to different WLANs. Connect a test client to each SSID and verify it gets an IP from the correct VLAN's DHCP scope.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Core WLC concept: WLANs map to VLANs via dynamic interfaces. Know the chain.

5.10.d.info:
The WLAN's General tab contains the Interface/Interface Group dropdown that maps the WLAN to a specific dynamic interface (and thus a VLAN).

GUI location: WLANs tab → click WLAN → General tab → Interface/Interface Group field.

What the dropdown shows:

  • All dynamic interfaces configured on the WLC (corp-data, guest-net, voice-vlan, etc.)
  • Optionally, interface groups (for load balancing clients across multiple VLANs)
  • The Management interface (usually not chosen for client data — but possible)

To change the mapping:

  1. Open the WLAN for editing
  2. General tab
  3. Click the Interface/Interface Group dropdown
  4. Select the correct dynamic interface
  5. Apply

Effect of changing the mapping:

  • Currently associated clients are typically kicked (may need to reconnect)
  • New clients get IPs from the new VLAN's DHCP scope
  • All new traffic flows out the dynamic interface's VLAN tag

Common mistake: Mapping all WLANs to the management interface instead of dedicated dynamic interfaces. Results: guest and corporate clients end up on the same VLAN, defeating segmentation.

Interface groups (advanced): When you need to place clients across multiple VLANs (e.g., load-balance 2000 employees across VLANs 10, 11, 12), create an Interface Group combining the three dynamic interfaces. Assign the group to the WLAN. WLC hashes clients across the VLANs.

Verification after mapping:

  • Connect a test client to the WLAN
  • Check the client's IP address — it should be in the mapped VLAN's subnet
  • WLC Monitor → Clients shows the VLAN the client was assigned
  • Client can reach VLAN-specific resources (and not cross-VLAN resources, assuming proper ACLs)

hack.memory: General tab → Interface/Interface Group dropdown = where you map WLAN to VLAN. Simple: pick the right dynamic interface, Apply. Interface groups = for load-balancing across multiple VLANs.
hack.practice: In a WLC simulator, edit a WLAN → General tab → note the Interface/Interface Group dropdown. Change the mapping and observe how new associations land in the new VLAN. Document the location so you can find it fast on an exam sim.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Know WHERE to make the interface mapping — General tab. Exam sims test this location.

5.10.d.info:
A subtle but critical requirement: the switch trunk connecting the WLC to the rest of the network must allow all VLANs the WLC maps WLANs to. If a VLAN is missing from the trunk's allowed list, clients on that WLAN can't reach the wired network.

Typical topology:

WLC  <-- trunk port -->  Switch  --  Wired Network

The trunk carries tagged traffic for every VLAN the WLC uses (management, AP-manager, each dynamic interface's VLAN).

Switch-side configuration for a WLC trunk:

interface GigabitEthernet0/24
 description Trunk to WLC
 switchport trunk encapsulation dot1q
 switchport mode trunk
 switchport trunk allowed vlan 1,10,20,50,100,200
 switchport nonegotiate
 spanning-tree portfast trunk

(Allowed VLANs: 1 = management, 10 = corp, 20 = staff, 50 = voice, 100 = guest, 200 = IoT.)

Common failure: Admin configures a new WLAN → VLAN 300, but forgets to add VLAN 300 to the trunk's allowed list. Clients get IPs on VLAN 300 via DHCP but can't reach anything beyond the WLC because the trunk drops their tagged traffic.

WLC-side configuration: The WLC's physical port connects to the switch trunk. The dynamic interface you create specifies the VLAN tag. The port must be configured as a trunk on both sides.

Troubleshooting checklist when wireless clients can't reach wired resources:

  1. WLAN status enabled?
  2. Dynamic interface created and has correct IP + VLAN?
  3. Switch trunk allows the VLAN? (show interface trunk)
  4. Native VLAN matches on both sides (if using native)?
  5. Switchport mode is trunk, not access?

Exam scenario: 'Clients get IPs but can't ping the gateway' → check switch trunk allowed VLANs. This is a classic WLC/switch integration issue.

hack.memory: Switch trunk to WLC MUST allow every VLAN the WLC uses. Add a new dynamic interface on VLAN 300? Add VLAN 300 to the trunk's allowed list. Forget this = clients get IPs but can't reach anything. Classic failure mode.
hack.practice: In a lab, set up a WLC → switch trunk. Map a WLAN to VLAN 50. Deliberately exclude VLAN 50 from the trunk's allowed list ('switchport trunk allowed vlan 1,10,100'). Clients associate, get IPs, but can't reach anything. Now add VLAN 50 to the allowed list — works. This shows the failure pattern vividly.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Classic exam troubleshooting scenario: check the switch trunk allowed VLANs when WLC clients can't reach wired resources.

5.10.e.info:
A WLAN with Status = Disabled exists in the WLC configuration but is not operationally active. APs do NOT broadcast the SSID in beacons, and clients cannot discover or connect to it.

What's running when disabled:

  • WLAN row exists in the WLC's WLAN list
  • Config is saved (security, interface mapping, all settings)
  • But no radio activity for this WLAN

What users experience:

  • SSID not visible in Wi-Fi scan results
  • Attempting to connect by manually typing the SSID fails (the WLC refuses association)
  • Existing associated clients are immediately kicked when the WLAN is disabled

Why disabled is the default for new WLANs: Safety. The GUI creates WLANs with Status = Disabled so admins can finish configuring security, VLAN mapping, and other settings before exposing the WLAN to clients. Prevents broadcasting a broken config.

How to tell from the GUI:

  • WLANs tab — the Status column shows 'Disabled' (red or grey) or 'Enabled' (green) per WLAN
  • Edit a WLAN — General tab shows a Status dropdown with current value

Administrative uses of Disable:

  • Temporarily disable a WLAN during maintenance (e.g., changing security settings)
  • Disable a seasonal WLAN (pop-up events, summer interns)
  • Pre-configure a WLAN and enable later for a scheduled go-live

Key exam fact: A new WLAN is created with Status = Disabled by default. Clients can't see it until you enable it.

hack.memory: Status: Disabled = WLAN exists but invisible to clients. Default state for new WLANs (safety). APs don't broadcast the SSID. Existing associations are kicked. Must flip Enabled for the network to go live.
hack.practice: Create a WLAN with perfect security + interface config but leave Status=Disabled. Scan from a client — SSID not visible. This is what the admin sees when they forget to enable a new WLAN. Internalize this pattern.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Key recall: new WLAN = disabled by default. Must enable to broadcast.

5.10.e.info:
Enabling a WLAN is the final step that makes all the other configuration actually take effect. Takes 3 clicks in the GUI.

GUI steps:

  1. WLANs tab → click the WLAN ID to open it for editing
  2. General tab
  3. Status dropdown → select Enabled
  4. Click Apply at the bottom

What happens immediately:

  • WLC sends new WLAN config to all associated APs (or to APs in the relevant AP group)
  • APs start including the SSID in their beacon frames (every ~100ms by default)
  • SSID appears in client Wi-Fi scan results within seconds
  • Clients can now associate and authenticate

Verification after enabling:

  • WLANs list → Status column shows 'Enabled' (green)
  • Wireless → APs → check that APs are 'Registered' and broadcasting the expected SSIDs
  • Scan from a wireless client — SSID visible
  • Connect a test client with the configured PSK — successful association
  • Monitor → Clients → the test client appears as 'Associated' and 'Authenticated'
  • Client has an IP from the mapped VLAN's DHCP scope

Disable vs delete:

  • Disable — temporary, all config retained, easy to re-enable
  • Delete — permanent, config lost, SSID removed from all APs

For maintenance or temporary shutdowns, always disable rather than delete.

Bulk enable/disable: Some WLC versions support selecting multiple WLANs and enabling/disabling in bulk. Useful for staged rollouts.

hack.memory: Enable WLAN = 3 clicks: General → Status: Enabled → Apply. APs start broadcasting within seconds. Verify: Status column shows Enabled, client can scan and connect, DHCP assigns correct IP. Disable for maintenance, delete for permanent removal.
hack.practice: In a WLC simulator, enable a disabled WLAN and verify: (1) Status column changes to Enabled, (2) client scan sees the SSID, (3) client associates and gets IP. Do this rollout sequence three times until it's muscle memory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. Exam sim may require enabling a pre-configured WLAN. Know the General tab location.

5.10.e.info:
The single most common troubleshooting scenario for newly deployed WLANs: 'Users can't see the new SSID.' And the single most common cause: the admin forgot to flip Status to Enabled.

Why this happens so often:

  • Creating a WLAN involves many steps: Profile, SSID, Security, Interface mapping — and Enable is the last, easy-to-skip step
  • Admins assume 'Apply' = 'live' — but Apply just saves the disabled config
  • No visible confirmation during SSID scan in the lab (admin usually tests later)
  • Screenshot-based tutorials sometimes skip the Enable step

Troubleshooting checklist for 'SSID not visible':

  1. Is the WLAN Status = Enabled? (Most common fix)
  2. Are APs registered with the WLC? (Wireless → APs tab)
  3. Is the WLAN associated with an AP group that includes the expected APs?
  4. Are APs in the coverage area of the test client?
  5. Are AP radios enabled (2.4 GHz / 5 GHz / 6 GHz as expected)?
  6. Is the client in range and scanning on the right bands?
  7. Is the SSID set to 'hidden' (not broadcast)?

Exam trap:

  • Question shows a perfectly configured WLAN with every security and interface setting correct
  • Users report they can't see the new SSID
  • Answer choices include complex security issues, AP registration, RF problems
  • Correct answer: WLAN Status = Disabled (the simplest, most-overlooked fix)

Lesson: When troubleshooting 'new SSID not visible,' check Enable FIRST. Don't waste time on complex theories until you've confirmed the basics.

hack.memory: New SSID not visible? FIRST check: WLAN Status = Enabled? This is the #1 rookie mistake and the #1 exam easy-point question. All other troubleshooting (AP registration, RF, security) comes after confirming the basic Enable.
hack.practice: Build a mental troubleshooting flowchart for 'SSID not visible.' Top of the tree: 'Is WLAN enabled?' If yes, branch down to AP registration, RF, client issues. If no, enable and retest. Always start at the top.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 57-58. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 29. High-probability exam troubleshooting question. Cheapest easy point on wireless: check Enable first.
